Nothing was mentioned in the wording of the verse regarding this name they mentioned. Rather, what the verse contains is that he had "knowledge of the Book" and that he said: "I will bring it to you." It is known, nonetheless, that the act is the act of Allah in reality. From all this, it is concluded that he had such knowledge of Allah and connection to Him that if he asked his Lord for something by turning to Him, He would not fail to respond. Or if you wish, say: if Allah, the Glorified, willed it.

It also becomes clear from the preceding that this knowledge was not of the category of discursive sciences (العلوم الفكرية) that accept acquisition and learning.

His saying: أَنَا آتِيكَ بِهِ قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ — "‘I will bring it to you before your gaze returns to you’" (27:40). The gaze (الطرف)—as has been said—is the glance and looking. The return of the gaze is the arrival of the thing looked at to the soul and the human’s knowledge of it. So the meaning is: I will bring it to you in less than the temporal interval between looking at a thing and knowing it.

It was said: the gaze is the movement of the eyelids and their opening for looking, and its return is their closing. Because it is a natural matter not dependent on intention, "returning" (الارتداد) was preferred over "sending back" (الرد); so it was said: "before your gaze returns to you" and not "before you send back your gaze." This is what was said.

But he erred. For the gaze, like breathing, is among the human’s voluntary acts (أفعال الإنسان الاختيارية), except that what prompts it is nature, as in breathing. Therefore, it does not require a prior deliberation (ترو) for its occurrence, as is required for things like eating and drinking. A voluntary act is that which is linked to the human’s will, and it is broader than that which is preceded by deliberation. What led this speaker into what he fell into was his assumption of equality between an act proceeding from choice (اختيار) and one proceeding from deliberation. Perhaps the subtle point in preferring "returning" over "sending back" is that the act, due to its not depending on deliberation, is as if it occurs by itself, not from a volition of the observer.

The address in His saying: "I will bring it to you before your gaze returns to you" is to Solomon (peace be upon him). He is the one who wants it brought to him, and he is the one to whom it is intended to be brought.

It was said: the address is to the afreet who said: "I will bring it to you before you rise from your place." The one who had knowledge of the Book, according to this speaker, is Solomon. He only said it to him to manifest the virtue of prophethood, and that what Allah empowered him with by teaching him a knowledge from the Book is greater than the power the afreet boasted of. So the meaning: Solomon said to the afreet when he said what he said: I will bring you the throne before the return of your gaze.

The author of al-Tafsir al-Kabir insisted on this view and cited points to support it, but they are poor points, and the original view does not suit the context, as we have alluded.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا رَآهُ مُسْتَقِرًّا عِنْدَهُ قَالَ هَذَا مِنْ فَضْلِ رَبِّي — "So when he saw it set before him, he said, ‘This is by the grace of my Lord...’" (27:40) to the end of the verse. That is, when Solomon saw the throne set before him, he said: This—meaning the presence of the throne and its being set before me in less than the twinkling of an eye—is from the favor of my Lord, without any merit from me, "to test me"—meaning to examine me—"whether I give thanks" for His blessing "or am ungrateful." "And whoever gives thanks, gives thanks only for his own sake"—meaning its benefit returns to him, not to my Lord. "And whoever is ungrateful"—and does not give thanks—"then my Lord is indeed All-sufficient, All-generous." In the end of the speech is an emphasis of what was in its beginning regarding the mention of favor.

It was said: the thing pointed to by "this" (هَذَا) is the empowerment to bring it, whether through an intermediary or by himself.

In this is the fact that the apparent sense of His saying: "So when he saw it set before him, he said," etc., is that this praise is linked to the state of seeing. What exists in the state of seeing is the presence of the throne before him, not the empowerment to bring it, which had been realized for some time.

In the speech, there is omission and brevity. The estimation is: Solomon permitted him to bring it thus, so he brought it as he said: "So when he saw it set before him." In the omission of what was omitted is a profound indication of the speed of the act, as if there were no interval at all between his claim to bring it thus and his seeing it set before him.

His saying, the Exalted: قَالَ نَكِّرُوا لَهَا عَرْشَهَا نَنْظُرْ أَتَهْتَدِي أَمْ تَكُونُ مِنَ الَّذِينَ لَا يَهْتَدُونَ — "He said, ‘Disguise her throne for her; let us see whether she is guided, or if she is one of those who are not guided’" (27:41). He said in al-Mufradat: Disguising (تنكير) a thing in terms of meaning is making it such that it is not recognized. The Exalted said: "Disguise her throne for her." Defining it (تعريفه) is making it such that it is recognized. [End of quote].

The context indicates that Solomon (peace be upon him) only said this when the Queen of Sheba and her assembly intended to come to him, when they entered upon him. He only wanted by that to test her intellect, just as he wanted by the original bringing of it to manifest a dazzling sign of the signs of his prophethood to her. Therefore, he commanded the disguising of the throne, then made His saying "let us see whether she is guided," etc., a consequence of it. The meaning is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: فَلَمَّا جَاءَتْ قِيلَ أَهَكَذَا عَرْشُكِ قَالَتْ كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ وَأُوتِينَا الْعِلْمَ مِنْ قَبْلِهَا وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ — "So when she came, it was said [to her], ‘Is your throne like this?’ She said, ‘It seems to be the same. We were given knowledge before it, and we were Muslims’" (27:42). That is, when the Queen came to Solomon (peace be upon him), it was said to her from Solomon’s side: "Is your throne like this?" which is a word of testing.

He did not say: "Is this your throne?" rather, the disguise was increased, so it was said: "Is your throne like this?" Thus, he asked about the similarity of her throne to this throne pointed to in its form and attributes. In this very sentence, there is a kind of disguise.

Her saying: "It seems to be the same" (كَأَنَّهُ هُوَ)—what she intended by it is that it is it. She only expressed it with the word of comparison (التشبيه) to guard against rashness and hastening to confirm without verification. Initial beliefs that have not been verified are often expressed through comparison.

Her saying: "We were given knowledge before it, and we were Muslims"—the pronoun in "before it" (قَبْلِهَا) refers to this sign, meaning the bringing of the throne, or to this state, meaning her seeing it after she came. The apparent context is that it is the completion of the Queen’s speech. When she saw the throne and was asked about its matter, she sensed that this was an allusion from them to what Allah had given Solomon of extraordinary power. So she answered by saying: "We were given knowledge before it," etc. That is, there is no need for this allusion and reminder; we already knew of his power before this sign or this state, and we were Muslims to Solomon, obedient to him.

It was said: her saying "We were given knowledge," etc., is from the speech of Solomon. It was said: from the speech of Solomon’s people. It was said: from the speech of the Queen, but the meaning is: we were given knowledge of the bringing of the throne before this state. These are all poor views.

His saying, the Exalted: وَصَدَّهَا مَا كَانَت تَّعْبُدُ مِن دُونِ اللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ "And what she used to worship besides Allah kept her from [the faith]. Indeed she belonged to a faithless people" (27:43). The term "keeping from" (الصد) denotes prevention and diversion. That which she was kept from was "submission to Allah" (الإسلام لله), which is what she would testify to when commanded to enter the palace, saying: أَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds" (27:44). As for her statement in the preceding verse, "and we had submitted" (وَكُنَّا مُسْلِمِينَ), it refers to her submission and compliance to Solomon (عليه السلام).

This is what the context of the verses yields; other commentators have proposed different interpretations for the meaning of the verse, which we have set aside.

His saying: إِنَّهَا كَانَتْ مِن قَوْمٍ كَافِرِينَ "Indeed she belonged to a faithless people" serves as the rationale for her being kept [from the faith]. The meaning is: that which she used to worship besides Allah—namely the sun, as mentioned previously in the report of the hoopoe—prevented her from submitting to Allah. The reason for this was that she belonged to a disbelieving people and followed them in their disbelief.

His saying, the Exalted: قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ "It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace’" (27:44) until the end of the verse. The palace (الصرح) refers to a castle or any elevated building; it also denotes an expansive, open area without a roof. A "deep pool" (اللجة) refers to a vast body of water. "Smoothed" (الممرد) is the passive participle of "to smooth" (التمريد), meaning to make level and sleek. "Crystal" (القوارير) refers to glass.

His saying: قِيلَ لَهَا ادْخُلِي الصَّرْحَ "It was said to her, ‘Enter the palace’"—it is as if the speaker was one of Solomon's servants in the presence of Solomon himself, one of those who would guide her to enter his presence, as is the custom when kings and dignitaries visit their peers.

His saying: فَلَمَّا رَأَتْهُ حَسِبَتْهُ لُجَّةً وَكَشَفَتْ عَنْ ساقَيْها "So when she saw it, she took it to be a deep pool, and bared her shanks." That is, when she saw the palace floor, she thought it was a deep pool of water because of the clarity of the glass, which resembled water. She bared her shanks by gathering her garments so that her hems would not get wet.

His saying: قالَ إِنَّهُ صَرْحٌ مُمَرَّدٌ مِنْ قَوارِيرَ "He said, ‘It is a palace paved with crystal.’" The speaker is Solomon, drawing her attention to the fact that it was not a pool of water, but rather a smoothed palace made of glass. When she witnessed the grandeur of Solomon's kingdom—having already witnessed the matter of the hoopoe, the rejection of the gift, and the bringing of her throne—she did not doubt that these were signs of his prophethood, achieved without mere worldly cunning or political maneuvering. At that moment, she said: رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي "My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself..."

His saying: قالَتْ رَبِّ إِنِّي ظَلَمْتُ نَفْسِي وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ رَبِّ الْعالَمِينَ "She said, ‘My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself, and I submit with Solomon to Allah, the Lord of all the worlds.’" She first appealed to her Lord by confessing her wrongdoing (الظلم), as she had not worshipped Allah from the beginning or from the moment she saw these signs. Then she testified to her submission (الإسلام) to Allah alongside Solomon.

In her saying: وَأَسْلَمْتُ مَعَ سُلَيْمانَ لِلَّهِ "and I submit with Solomon to Allah," there is a transition (التفات) regarding the Exalted from the second person to the third person. The reason for this is the shift from a general faith in Allah—when she said, "My Lord! Indeed I have wronged myself"—to explicit monotheism (التوحيد). She testifies that her submission to Allah is alongside Solomon, following the path of Solomon's submission, which is monotheism (التوحيد). She then reinforces this declaration by describing the Exalted as "the Lord of all the worlds" (رب العالمين), meaning there is no lord for any part of the worlds other than Him. This is the monotheism of Lordship (توحيد الربوبية), which necessitates the monotheism of worship (توحيد العبادة)—a concept that no polytheist accepts.

(A Discussion on the Story of Solomon (عليه السلام))

1. What is mentioned of his stories in the Quran: Only a small portion of his stories (عليه السلام) is mentioned in the Noble Quran; however, reflection upon them guides one to the general scope of his history and the manifestations of his noble personality.

Among these is his inheriting from his father, David. The Exalted said: وَوَهَبْنَا لِدَاوُودَ سُلَيْمَانَ "And We gave Solomon to David" (38:30), and He said: وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ "Solomon inherited from David" (24:16).

Another is the granting of a magnificent kingdom to him, the subjugation of the jinn, birds, and wind for him, and his being taught the speech of birds. The mention of these blessings is repeated in the speech of the Exalted, as in Surah al-Baqarah (2:102), al-Anbiya (21:81), al-Naml (27:16-18), Saba (34:12-13), and Sad (38:35-39).

Also included is the reference to the casting of a [lifeless] body upon his throne, as in Surah Sad (38:34).

Another is the reference to the display of the "well-bred horses" (الصافنات الجياد) before him, as in Surah Sad (38:31-33).

Another is the reference to his being given understanding of the judgment regarding the sheep that strayed into the tilth, as in Surah al-Anbiya (21:78-79).

Another is the reference to the account of the ant, as in Surah al-Naml (27:18-19).

Another is the story of the hoopoe and the subsequent account of his dealings with the Queen of Sheba in Surah al-Naml (27:20-44).

Finally, there is the reference to the manner of his death (عليه السلام), as in Surah Saba (34:14).

We have provided the discussion pertaining to each of these stories in the commentary on the verses referring to them throughout this book.

2. His praise in the Quran: His name (عليه السلام) appears in over ten places in the speech of the Exalted, who praised him extensively, calling him a "penitent servant." The Exalted said: نِعْمَ الْعَبْدُ ۖ إِنَّهُ أَوَّابٌ "What an excellent servant! Indeed he was a penitent [soul]" (38:30). He described him with knowledge and judgment, saying: فَفَهَّمْنَاهَا سُلَيْمَانَ ۚ وَكُلًّا آتَيْنَا حُكْمًا وَعِلْمًا "We gave its understanding to Solomon, and to each We gave judgment and knowledge" (21:79). He also said: وَلَقَدْ آتَيْنَا دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ عِلْمًا "Certainly We gave David and Solomon knowledge" (27:15), and: وَقَالَ يَا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ عُلِّمْنَا مَنطِقَ الطَّيْرِ "and he said, ‘O people, we have been taught the speech of the birds’" (27:16). He counted him among the guided prophets, saying: وَأَيُّوبَ وَيُونُسَ وَهَارُونَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ "and Job, Jonah, Aaron, and Solomon" (4:163), and: وَنُوحًا هَدَيْنَا مِن قَبْلُ ۖ وَمِن ذُرِّيَّتِهِ دَاوُودَ وَسُلَيْمَانَ "and Noah We had guided before, and from his offspring, David and Solomon" (6:84).

3. Mention of him (عليه السلام) in the Old Testament: His story occurs in the First Book of Kings, where it elaborates on his retinue, the majesty of his affairs, the vastness of his kingdom, the abundance of his wealth, and the extent of his wisdom. However, it mentions none of his stories referred to in the Quran, except for the account that when the Queen of Sheba heard of Solomon's fame, his building of the House of the Lord in Jerusalem, and the wisdom granted to him, she came to him with many gifts, met him, and asked him questions to test him, which he answered, after which she returned (1).

The Old Testament speaks ill of him (عليه السلام), claiming (2) that toward the end of his life, he turned away from the worship of God to the worship of idols, prostrating to idols worshipped by some of his wives.

It also mentions that his mother was the wife of Uriah the Hittite, and that David (عليه السلام) fell in love with her and committed adultery with her. She became pregnant by him, and he plotted to have her husband, Uriah, killed until he was slain in battle. David then took her among his wives, and she became pregnant a second time and bore him Solomon.

(1) First Kings, Chapter 10. (2) Second Samuel, Chapters 11 and 12.

The Noble Quran exonerates him (عليه السلام) from the first of these two accusations by the same means it exonerates all prophets—by explicitly stating their guidance and infallibility (العصمة). Regarding him specifically, it says: وَمَا كَفَرَ سُلَيْمَانُ "Solomon was not faithless" (2:102).

As for the second accusation, he is exonerated by what the Quran narrates of his prayer when he heard the speech of the ant: رَبِّ أَوْزِعْنِي أَنْ أَشْكُرَ نِعْمَتَكَ الَّتِي أَنْعَمْتَ عَلَيَّ وَعَلى والِدَيَّ "My Lord! Inspire me to give thanks for Your blessing with which You have blessed me and my parents" (27:19). We have explained in its commentary that this indicates his mother was among the people of the "straight path" (الصراط المستقيم) whom Allah has blessed—the prophets, the truthful, the martyrs, and the righteous.

4. Narrations regarding his stories (عليه السلام): The reports narrated concerning his stories, especially the story of the hoopoe and the subsequent accounts of the Queen of Sheba, mostly contain strange matters that find few parallels even in mythical legends. Sound reason rejects them, and definitive history belies them. The most exaggerated of these are narrated from the likes of Ka'b and Wahb.

They reached such a level of exaggeration as to narrate that he ruled the entire earth, that his reign lasted seven hundred years, and that all humans, jinn, wild beasts, and birds were his soldiers. They claimed that in his assembly, six hundred thousand chairs were placed around his throne, upon which sat thousands of prophets and hundreds of thousands of princes from among the humans and jinn.

They also claimed that the mother of the Queen of Sheba was a jinni, and that her foot was like the hoof of a donkey, which she would hide from onlookers until she bared her shanks to enter the palace and her secret was revealed. Regarding her power, they claimed she had four hundred kings under her command, each ruling a province, and each king had four hundred thousand fighters. She supposedly had three hundred ministers managing her kingdom and twelve thousand commanders, each with twelve thousand fighters under him—among other wondrous reports that we can only categorize as Isra'iliyyat and thus disregard (1).

(1) Whoever wishes to examine them should refer to the collections of narrations such as al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Ara'is, al-Bihar, and the lengthy commentaries.

(Narrative Discussion)

In al-Ihtijaj, Abdullah ibn al-Hasan narrated with his chain from his forefathers (عليه السلام): When Abu Bakr resolved to prevent Fatimah (عليه السلام) from [possessing] Fadak and this reached her, she came to him and said: "O son of Abi Quhafah! Is it in the Book of Allah that you inherit from your father while I do not inherit from mine? You have brought a monstrous thing! Have you intentionally abandoned the Book of Allah and cast it behind your backs when it says: وَوَرِثَ سُلَيْمَانُ دَاوُودَ 'Solomon inherited from David'?" — the tradition continues.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, in the narration of Abu al-Jarud from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام) regarding the saying of the Mighty and Majestic: فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ "so they were held in check" (27:17), he said: "The first of them were held back for the last of them."

In al-Ihtijaj, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام) in a tradition, he said: "The word 'waiting' (الناظرة) in some dialects means 'expecting' (المنتظرة). Have you not heard His saying: فَناظِرَةٌ بِمَ يَرْجِعُ الْمُرْسَلُونَ 'and see what the envoys bring back' (27:35)?"

In al-Basa'ir, with its chain from Jabir from Abu Ja'far (عليه السلام), he said: "Indeed, the Greatest Name of Allah (اسم الله الأعظم) consists of seventy-three letters. Asaph possessed only one letter of it. He spoke it, and the earth between him and the throne of Bilqis sank away, then he reached out and took the throne with his hand, and the earth returned to its original state faster than the blink of an eye. We possess seventy-two letters of the Name, and one letter remains with Allah, which He has reserved for Himself in the knowledge of the Unseen (علم الغيب). And there is no power or strength except by Allah, the All-High, the All-Great."

I say: This meaning is also narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام), and it is narrated in al-Kafi from Jabir from Abu Ja'far, and from al-Nawfali from Abu al-Hasan, the Master of the Military (عليه السلام).

The statement that "the Greatest Name is such-and-such letters and Asaph had one letter which he spoke" does not contradict what we previously established—that this Name is not of the nature of spoken words. The very context indicates that "letter" (الحرف) refers to something other than a phonetic letter; it is expressed this way because the common understanding of a "name" among people is a verbal name composed of spoken letters.

In al-Majma', regarding the saying of the Exalted: قَبْلَ أَنْ يَرْتَدَّ إِلَيْكَ طَرْفُكَ "before your glance returns to you" (27:40), several views were mentioned—until he said—the fifth is that the earth was "folded" (طويت) for him, and this is what is narrated from Abu Abdillah (عليه السلام).

I say: What is narrated regarding "folding" (الطي) is not different from what was previously narrated regarding "sinking" (الخسف).

The other views he cited were five: First, that the angels carried it to him. Second, that the wind carried it. Third, that Allah created successive movements in it. Fourth, that it broke through its place there and then emerged before Solomon. Fifth, that Allah caused it to cease to exist in its place and recreated it in Solomon's assembly.

There is another view mentioned by some: that existence is the "renewal of likes" (تجدد الأمثال) through His creation; Allah bestowed existence upon her throne in Sheba, and then in the following instant, He bestowed it in the presence of Solomon. These views range from the impossible—such as the fifth—to those for which there is no evidence, like the rest.

In the same source, al-Ayyashi narrated in his Tafsir with a chain: Musa ibn Muhammad ibn Ali ibn Musa and Yahya ibn Aktham met, and the latter questioned him. Musa said: "I entered upon my brother, Ali ibn Muhammad (عليه السلام), and we exchanged exhortations until I reached the point of obedience to him. I said to him: 'May I be your ransom! Ibn Aktham asked me questions for which I should provide a verdict.' He laughed and said: 'Did you give him a verdict?' I said: 'No.' He asked: 'Why?' I replied: 'I did not know them.' He said: 'What are they?' I said: 'He said: Tell me about Solomon—was he in need of the knowledge of Asaph ibn Barkhiya?'" Then I mentioned the other questions.

He said: "Write, my brother: In the Name of Allah, the All-Beneficent, the Most Merciful. You asked about the saying of Allah, the Exalted, in His Book: قَالَ الَّذِي عِندَهُ عِلْمٌ مِّنَ الْكِتَابِ 'The one who had knowledge of the Book said' (27:40). That was Asaph ibn Barkhiya. Solomon was not incapable of knowing what Asaph knew, but he wished for his community of jinn and humans to know that Asaph was the Proof (الحجة) after him. That was from the knowledge of Solomon which he entrusted to Asaph by the command of Allah, so Allah gave him understanding of it so that there would be no dispute regarding his leadership (الإمامة) and his status as a guide—just as Solomon was given understanding during the life of David so that his leadership and prophethood would be recognized after him, to reinforce the Proof (الحجة) over creation."

I say: This narration was cited in Ruh al-Ma'ani from al-Majma', after which the author said: "It is as you see," meaning he saw no merit in it. I see no reason for his objection other than that he encountered the mention of leadership (الإمامة) in it and it did not please him.

In Nur al-Thaqalayn, from al-Kafi, from the Commander of the Faithful (عليه السلام), he said: "Be more hopeful for that which you do not expect than for that which you do expect..."—until he said—"and the Queen of Sheba went forth and submitted with Solomon (عليه السلام)."

Ayahs

وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنَآ إِلَىٰ ثَمُودَ أَخَاهُمْ صَـٰلِحًا أَنِ ٱعْبُدُوا۟ ٱللَّهَ فَإِذَا هُمْ فَرِيقَانِ يَخْتَصِمُونَ (٤٥)
Certainly We sent to Thamud Salih, their brother, [with the summons:] ‘Worship Allah!’ But thereat they became two groups contending with each other. (45)
قَالَ يَـٰقَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ ٱلْحَسَنَةِ ۖ لَوْلَا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ ٱللَّهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (٤٦)
He said, ‘O My people! Why do you press for evil sooner than for good? Why do you not plead to Allah for forgiveness so that you may receive His mercy?’ (46)
قَالُوا۟ ٱطَّيَّرْنَا بِكَ وَبِمَن مَّعَكَ ۚ قَالَ طَـٰٓئِرُكُمْ عِندَ ٱللَّهِ ۖ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ (٤٧)
They said, ‘We take you and those who are with you for a bad omen.’ He said, ‘Your bad omens are from Allah. Indeed, you are a people being tested.’ (47)
وَكَانَ فِى ٱلْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ يُفْسِدُونَ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَلَا يُصْلِحُونَ (٤٨)
There were nine persons in the city who caused corruption in the land and did not set things right. (48)
قَالُوا۟ تَقَاسَمُوا۟ بِٱللَّهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُۥ وَأَهْلَهُۥ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِۦ مَا شَهِدْنَا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِۦ وَإِنَّا لَصَـٰدِقُونَ (٤٩)
They said, ‘Swear by Allah that we will attack him and his family by night. Then we will tell his heir that we were not present at the murder of his family and that we speak the truth.’ (49)
وَمَكَرُوا۟ مَكْرًا وَمَكَرْنَا مَكْرًا وَهُمْ لَا يَشْعُرُونَ (٥٠)
They devised a plot, and We [too] devised a plan, but they were not aware. (50)
فَٱنظُرْ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ أَنَّا دَمَّرْنَـٰهُمْ وَقَوْمَهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (٥١)
So observe how was the outcome of their plotting, as We destroyed them and all their people. (51)
فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خَاوِيَةًۢ بِمَا ظَلَمُوٓا۟ ۗ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَةً لِّقَوْمٍ يَعْلَمُونَ (٥٢)
So there lay their houses, fallen in ruin because of their wrongdoing. There is indeed a sign in that for a people who have knowledge. (52)
وَأَنجَيْنَا ٱلَّذِينَ ءَامَنُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ يَتَّقُونَ (٥٣)
And We delivered those who had faith and were Godwary. (53)

(Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of the Prophet Salih (عليه السلام) and his people. The aspect of warning (الإنذار) in these verses prevails over the aspect of glad tidings (التبشير), as previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلَقَدْ أَرْسَلْنا إِلى ثَمُودَ أَخاهُمْ صالِحاً — until His saying — يَخْتَصِمُونَ (Certainly We sent to Thamud their brother Salih, [saying], ‘Worship Allah!’ Behold, they became two factions at odds with each other. 27:45). Contention (الاختصام) and disputation (التخاصم) refer to quarreling (التنازع). The dual noun "two factions" (فَرِيقانِ) is described with a plural verb in His saying "at odds with each other" (يَخْتَصِمُونَ) because the intended meaning of the two factions is the entire community (الأمة). The word "Behold" (فَإِذا) indicates a sudden event (فجائية).

The meaning is: I swear, We certainly sent to the people of Thamud their brother and kinsman Salih. It was hoped that they would unite upon faith (الإيمان), but suddenly they split into two factions—believers and disbelievers—contending and quarreling over the truth, each saying, "The truth is with me." Perhaps their contention refers to what Allah narrated about them elsewhere in His saying: قالَ الْمَلَأُ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا مِنْ قَوْمِهِ لِلَّذِينَ اسْتُضْعِفُوا لِمَنْ آمَنَ مِنْهُمْ أَتَعْلَمُونَ أَنَّ صالِحاً مُرْسَلٌ مِنْ رَبِّهِ قالُوا إِنَّا بِما أُرْسِلَ بِهِ مُؤْمِنُونَ قالَ الَّذِينَ اسْتَكْبَرُوا إِنَّا بِالَّذِي آمَنْتُمْ بِهِ كافِرُونَ (The elite of his people who were arrogant said to those who were oppressed—to those among them who had faith—‘Do you know that Salih has been sent by his Lord?’ They said, ‘We indeed believe in what he has been sent with.’ Those who were arrogant said, ‘We indeed disbelieve in what you believe in.’ 7:75-76).

From this, it becomes clear that one of the two factions was a group of the oppressed (المستضعفين) who believed in him, while the other consisted of the arrogant (المستكبرون) and the remaining oppressed who followed their leaders.

His saying, the Exalted: قالَ يا قَوْمِ لِمَ تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ قَبْلَ الْحَسَنَةِ (He said, ‘O my people! Why do you seek to hasten the evil before the good? 27:46) etc. Hastening the evil (السيئة) before the good (الحسنة) means rushing to ask for punishment (العذاب) before seeking the mercy (الرحمة) that is brought about by faith and seeking forgiveness (الاستغفار).

Through this, it appears that Salih (عليه السلام) only rebuked them with these words after they had hamstrung the she-camel and said to him: يا صالِحُ ائْتِنا بِما تَعِدُنا إِنْ كُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُرْسَلِينَ (‘O Salih, bring us what you threaten us with, if you are one of the apostles.’ 7:77). Thus, his saying: لَوْ لا تَسْتَغْفِرُونَ اللهَ لَعَلَّكُمْ تُرْحَمُونَ (Why do you not plead to Allah for forgiveness, so that you may receive His mercy?’ 27:46) is an exhortation (تحضيض) toward faith and repentance (التوبة), so that Allah might have mercy on them and remove the punishment He had promised them—a promise that was not a lie.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا اطَّيَّرْنا بِكَ وَبِمَنْ مَعَكَ قالَ طائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ (They said, ‘We take you and those who are with you for an ill omen.’ He said, ‘Your omen is with Allah. 27:47) etc. Taking an ill omen (التطير) is to see something as a bad portent (التشؤم). They used to frequently take omens from birds (الطير), which is why the act of seeing a bad portent was called tataiyur (التطير). A person's portion of evil was referred to as a "bird" (طائراً), as has been said.

So their statement addressing Salih: اطَّيَّرْنا بِكَ وَبِمَنْ مَعَكَ ("We take you and those who are with you for an ill omen") means: We see a bad portent in you and those who have believed in you and stayed with you, because your undertaking of the mission and their faith in you coincided with the trials and calamities we have been afflicted with; therefore, we will not believe in you.

His statement addressing the people: طائِرُكُمْ عِنْدَ اللهِ ("Your omen is with Allah") means: Your portion of evil—which is the punishment necessitated by your deeds—is with Allah, the Glorified.

For this reason, He turned away from the statement "Your omen is with Allah" by saying: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ (Rather you are a people being tested.’ 27:47), meaning you are being tried (تختبرون) with good and evil so that the believer among you may be distinguished from the disbeliever, and the obedient from the disobedient.

The meaning of the verse is: The people said, "We see a bad omen in you, O Salih, and in those with you, so we will neither believe nor seek forgiveness." Salih replied, "Your 'bird'—which contains your portion of evil—is with Allah; it is the record of your deeds. Neither I nor those with me have any effect upon you such that we would drive these trials toward you. Rather, you are a people being tested and tried through these matters so that the believer may be distinguished from the disbeliever, and the obedient from the disobedient."

It has also been said that the "bird" (الطائر) is the cause from which a person receives whatever good or evil befalls him. Just as they took ill omens from birds, they also took good omens from them. To them, the "bird" was that which confronts a person with good or evil, as in His saying, the Exalted: وَكُلَّ إِنسانٍ أَلْزَمْناهُ طائِرَهُ فِي عُنُقِهِ وَنُخْرِجُ لَهُ يَوْمَ الْقِيامَةِ كِتاباً (We have fastened every man’s omen to his neck, and on the Day of Resurrection We shall bring out for him a book 17:13). Since whatever good or evil confronts a person is by the decree (قضاء) of Allah, the Glorified, written in a book, the "bird" is the Preserved Book (الكتاب) in which what is destined for man is ordained.

However, the apparent meaning of the end of the verse in Surah al-Isra is that the "bird" refers to the record of deeds (كتاب الأعمال) rather than the book of decree (كتاب القضاء), as indicated by His saying: اقْرَأْ كِتابَكَ كَفى بِنَفْسِكَ الْيَوْمَ عَلَيْكَ حَسِيباً (‘Read your book! Today your own soul suffices as your reckoner.’ 17:14).

It has also been said that the meaning of بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تُفْتَنُونَ is "rather you are a people being punished," but what we mentioned first is more appropriate.

His saying, the Exalted: وَكانَ فِي الْمَدِينَةِ تِسْعَةُ رَهْطٍ (There were nine cliques in the city 27:48) etc. Al-Raghib said: A clique (الرهط) is a group (العصابة) of fewer than ten, and it is said up to forty. It has also been said that the difference between raht (الرهط) and nafar (النفر) is that raht is from three or seven up to ten, while nafar is from three to nine.

It is said that raht here refers to individuals, which is why it serves as the specification (تمييز) for the number nine, as it carries the meaning of a plural; thus, those who took the oath were nine men.

His saying, the Exalted: قالُوا تَقاسَمُوا بِاللهِ لَنُبَيِّتَنَّهُ وَأَهْلَهُ ثُمَّ لَنَقُولَنَّ لِوَلِيِّهِ ما شَهِدْنا مَهْلِكَ أَهْلِهِ وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ (They said, ‘Swear to one another by Allah: We will surely attack him and his family by night, then we will tell his blood-wit guardian, “We were not present at the destruction of his family, and we are indeed truthful.” ’ 27:49). Taking an oath among one another (التقاسم) is to participate in a collective swearing. Attacking by night (التبييت) is to intend harm during the night. A man's family (أهل) are those joined with him by house, lineage, or religion. Perhaps the intended meaning of his family here is his wife and children, given the context of the subsequent phrase: "then we will tell his blood-wit guardian (لِوَلِيِّهِ) 'We were not present...'" His saying: "and we are indeed truthful" (وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ) is coordinated with "We were not present" (ما شَهِدْنا), thus forming part of what was said.

The meaning is: The corrupt clique said, having sworn to one another by Allah: "We will surely kill him and his family by night, then we will say to his guardian—when he follows up and seeks vengeance—'We did not witness the destruction of his family, and we are indeed truthful in this statement.'" Denying the witnessing of the destruction of his family is a denial of witnessing his own destruction by implication or by greater reason (الأولوية), as has been said.

It has also been suggested that His saying: "and we are indeed truthful" (وَإِنَّا لَصادِقُونَ) is a circumstantial clause (حال) referring to the subject of "we will say," meaning: We will say such to his guardian while we are truthful in this statement, because we witnessed the destruction of both him and his family together, not the destruction of his family alone.

The forced nature (التكلف) of this interpretation is not hidden, and it has been justified in other ways even more forced than this; there is no necessity to consider it a circumstantial clause in the first place.

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَكَرُوا مَكْراً وَمَكَرْنا مَكْراً وَهُمْ لا يَشْعُرُونَ (They devised a stratagem, and We [too] devised a stratagem, while they were not aware. 27:50). As for their stratagem (مكرهم), it was the conspiracy to attack him and his family by night and the taking of the oath, as evidenced by the preceding context. As for the stratagem of Allah (مكره تعالى), it was His decreeing the destruction of them all, as evidenced by the subsequent context.

His saying, the Exalted: فَانْظُرْ كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ مَكْرِهِمْ أَنَّا دَمَّرْناهُمْ وَقَوْمَهُمْ أَجْمَعِينَ (So observe how was the outcome of their stratagem: that We destroyed them and their people, all together. 27:51). Destruction (التدمير) is annihilation (الإهلاك). The plural pronouns refer to the clique. The fact that the outcome of their stratagem was the destruction of them and their people is because their stratagem invited the Divine stratagem by way of retribution (المجازاة), which necessitated the destruction of them and their people.

His saying, the Exalted: فَتِلْكَ بُيُوتُهُمْ خاوِيَةً بِما ظَلَمُوا (So there are their houses, empty because of their wrongdoing. 27:52) etc. Empty (الخاوية) means vacant, derived from khawa’ (الخواء) meaning emptiness (الخلاء). The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْجَيْنَا الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ (And We delivered those who had faith and were God-conscious. 27:53). In this is a glad tiding for the believers of deliverance (الإنجاء). He followed it with His saying: "and were God-conscious" (وَكانُوا يَتَّقُونَ) because God-consciousness (التقوى) — the internal shield of piety and protection against sin — is like a shield for faith. Indeed, the Exalted has said: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلْمُتَّقِينَ (and the outcome will be in favor of the God-conscious. 7:128) and He said: وَالْعاقِبَةُ لِلتَّقْوى (and the [good] outcome is for God-consciousness. 20:132).

Ayahs

وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِۦٓ أَتَأْتُونَ ٱلْفَـٰحِشَةَ وَأَنتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ (٥٤)
[We also sent] Lot, when he said to his people, ‘What! Do you commit this indecency while you look on? (54)
أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ ٱلرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِّن دُونِ ٱلنِّسَآءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ (٥٥)
Do you approach men with [sexual] desire instead of women?! Indeed, you are a senseless lot!’ (55)
۞ فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِۦٓ إِلَّآ أَن قَالُوٓا۟ أَخْرِجُوٓا۟ ءَالَ لُوطٍ مِّن قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ (٥٦)
But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel Lot’s family from your town! They are indeed a puritanical lot.’ (56)
فَأَنجَيْنَـٰهُ وَأَهْلَهُۥٓ إِلَّا ٱمْرَأَتَهُۥ قَدَّرْنَـٰهَا مِنَ ٱلْغَـٰبِرِينَ (٥٧)
So We delivered him and his family, except his wife. We ordained her to be among those who remained behind. (57)
وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِم مَّطَرًا ۖ فَسَآءَ مَطَرُ ٱلْمُنذَرِينَ (٥٨)
Then We poured down upon them a rain [of stones]. Evil was that rain for those who had been warned! (58)

(Commentary)

This is a summary of the story of Lot (عليه‌السلام), which, like the preceding story, is characterized by the predominance of warning (الإنذار) over glad tidings (التبشير).

His saying, the Exalted: وَلُوطًا إِذْ قَالَ لِقَوْمِهِ أَتَأْتُونَ الْفَاحِشَةَ وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ "And Lot, when he said to his people, ‘Do you commit this indecency while you look on?’" (27:54). This is coordinated (معطوف) with the position of "We sent" (أرسلنا) in the previous story by means of an implied verb; the estimated meaning being: "And We certainly sent Lot." This has been suggested, though it is also possible that it is coordinated with the core of the narrative by implying the command "Mention" (اذكر). The term indecency (الفاحشة) refers to a trait reaching the utmost limit of repulsiveness (الشناعة), and what is intended here is sodomy.

His saying: وَأَنْتُمْ تُبْصِرُونَ "while you look on" means: while you are in a state where you see one another and look at one another during the act of indecency. This is similar to His saying in another place: وَتَأْتُونَ فِي نَادِيكُمُ الْمُنْكَرَ "and commit abominations in your gatherings?" (29:29). It has also been said that it refers to the "sight of the heart" (إبصار القلب), meaning their knowledge of its repulsiveness, but this is far-fetched.

His saying, the Exalted: أَئِنَّكُمْ لَتَأْتُونَ الرِّجَالَ شَهْوَةً مِنْ دُونِ النِّسَاءِ ۚ بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ "Do you approach men with desire instead of women? Rather, you are a senseless people." (27:55). The interrogative (الاستفهام) here is for denunciation (الإنكار). The inclusion of the two instruments of emphasis—the particle inna (إن) and the lam (اللام)—within the interrogative sentence serves to indicate that the content of the sentence is so far-fetched (الاستبعاد) that no one would believe it. In any case, the sentence serves as an explanation (التفسير) of the "indecency."

His saying: بَلْ أَنْتُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ "Rather, you are a senseless people" means: you are persistent in ignorance (الجهل); there is no benefit in rebuking you or denouncing you, for you are not to be deterred. The use of the second-person address تَجْهَلُونَ "you are senseless" in place of the third-person "they are senseless" is an instance of placing the effect (المسبب) in the position of the cause (السبب), as if it were said: "Rather, they are a people who are ignorant, and thus you are acting ignorantly."

His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا كَانَ جَوَابَ قَوْمِهِ إِلَّا أَنْ قَالُوا أَخْرِجُوا آلَ لُوطٍ مِنْ قَرْيَتِكُمْ ۖ إِنَّهُمْ أُنَاسٌ يَتَطَهَّرُونَ "But the only answer of his people was that they said, ‘Expel Lot’s family from your town! They are indeed a purist lot.’" (27:56). That is, they keep themselves pure from this act; this was said by way of mockery (الاستهزاء).

His saying, the Exalted: فَأَنْجَيْنَاهُ وَأَهْلَهُ إِلَّا امْرَأَتَهُ قَدَّرْنَاهَا مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ "So We delivered him and his family, except his wife: We ordained her to be among those who remained behind." (27:57). What is meant by "his family" (أهله) is the people of his household, due to His saying, the Exalted: فَمَا وَجَدْنَا فِيهَا غَيْرَ بَيْتٍ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ "but We did not find in it any but one house of Muslims." (51:36). His saying: قَدَّرْنَاهَا مِنَ الْغَابِرِينَ "We ordained her to be among those who remained behind" means: We made her among those who stayed behind to face the punishment.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ مَطَرًا ۖ فَسَاءَ مَطَرُ الْمُنْذَرِينَ "And We rained down upon them a rain: evil was the rain of those who were warned." (27:58). What is meant by "rain" (المطر) is stones of baked clay (سجيل), due to His saying, the Exalted: وَأَمْطَرْنَا عَلَيْهِمْ حِجَارَةً مِنْ سِجِّيلٍ "and rained on them stones of baked clay." (15:74). Thus, His saying: مَطَرًا "a rain," by being indefinite (التنكير), indicates its specific nature (النوعية)—that is, We sent down upon them a rain of momentous significance.

Ayahs

قُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلَـٰمٌ عَلَىٰ عِبَادِهِ ٱلَّذِينَ ٱصْطَفَىٰٓ ۗ ءَآللَّهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٥٩)
Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and Peace be to the servants whom He has chosen.’ Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]? (59)
أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضَ وَأَنزَلَ لَكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ مَآءً فَأَنۢبَتْنَا بِهِۦ حَدَآئِقَ ذَاتَ بَهْجَةٍ مَّا كَانَ لَكُمْ أَن تُنۢبِتُوا۟ شَجَرَهَآ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ (٦٠)
Is He who created the heavens and the earth, and sends down for you water from the sky, whereby We grow delightful gardens, whose trees you could never cause to grow...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, they are a lot who equate [others with Allah]. (60)
أَمَّن جَعَلَ ٱلْأَرْضَ قَرَارًا وَجَعَلَ خِلَـٰلَهَآ أَنْهَـٰرًا وَجَعَلَ لَهَا رَوَٰسِىَ وَجَعَلَ بَيْنَ ٱلْبَحْرَيْنِ حَاجِزًا ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٦١)
Is He who made the earth an abode [for you], and made rivers [flowing] through it, and set firm mountains for it [’s stability], and set a barrier between the two seas...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Indeed, most of them do not know. (61)
أَمَّن يُجِيبُ ٱلْمُضْطَرَّ إِذَا دَعَاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ ٱلسُّوٓءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفَآءَ ٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قَلِيلًا مَّا تَذَكَّرُونَ (٦٢)
Is He who answers the call of the distressed [person] when he invokes Him and removes his distress, and makes you successors on the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the admonition that you take. (62)
أَمَّن يَهْدِيكُمْ فِى ظُلُمَـٰتِ ٱلْبَرِّ وَٱلْبَحْرِ وَمَن يُرْسِلُ ٱلرِّيَـٰحَ بُشْرًۢا بَيْنَ يَدَىْ رَحْمَتِهِۦٓ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ تَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (٦٣)
Is He who guides you in the darkness of land and sea and who sends the winds as harbingers of His mercy...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]. (63)
أَمَّن يَبْدَؤُا۟ ٱلْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُۥ وَمَن يَرْزُقُكُم مِّنَ ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ۗ أَءِلَـٰهٌ مَّعَ ٱللَّهِ ۚ قُلْ هَاتُوا۟ بُرْهَـٰنَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٦٤)
Is He who originates the creation, then He will bring it back, and who provides for you from the sky and the earth...? What! Is there a god besides Allah? Say, ‘Produce your evidence, if you are truthful.’ (64)
قُل لَّا يَعْلَمُ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ ٱلْغَيْبَ إِلَّا ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَمَا يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (٦٥)
Say, ‘No one in the heavens or the earth knows the Unseen except Allah, and they are not aware when they will be resurrected.’ (65)
بَلِ ٱدَّٰرَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِى ٱلْـَٔاخِرَةِ ۚ بَلْ هُمْ فِى شَكٍّ مِّنْهَا ۖ بَلْ هُم مِّنْهَا عَمُونَ (٦٦)
Do they comprehend the knowledge of the Hereafter? No, they are in doubt about it. Indeed, they are blind to it. (66)
وَقَالَ ٱلَّذِينَ كَفَرُوٓا۟ أَءِذَا كُنَّا تُرَٰبًا وَءَابَآؤُنَآ أَئِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ (٦٧)
The faithless say, ‘What! When we and our fathers have become dust shall we be raised [from the dead]? (67)
لَقَدْ وُعِدْنَا هَـٰذَا نَحْنُ وَءَابَآؤُنَا مِن قَبْلُ إِنْ هَـٰذَآ إِلَّآ أَسَـٰطِيرُ ٱلْأَوَّلِينَ (٦٨)
We and our fathers were promised this before. [But] these are just myths of the ancients.’ (68)
قُلْ سِيرُوا۟ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ فَٱنظُرُوا۟ كَيْفَ كَانَ عَـٰقِبَةُ ٱلْمُجْرِمِينَ (٦٩)
Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the guilty.’ (69)
وَلَا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلَا تَكُن فِى ضَيْقٍ مِّمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ (٧٠)
Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their guile. (70)
وَيَقُولُونَ مَتَىٰ هَـٰذَا ٱلْوَعْدُ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (٧١)
They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (71)
قُلْ عَسَىٰٓ أَن يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُم بَعْضُ ٱلَّذِى تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ (٧٢)
Say, ‘Perhaps there is right behind you some of what you seek to hasten.’ (72)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى ٱلنَّاسِ وَلَـٰكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لَا يَشْكُرُونَ (٧٣)
Indeed your Lord is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (73)
وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ مَا تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَمَا يُعْلِنُونَ (٧٤)
Your Lord knows whatever their breasts conceal, and whatever they disclose. (74)
وَمَا مِنْ غَآئِبَةٍ فِى ٱلسَّمَآءِ وَٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِى كِتَـٰبٍ مُّبِينٍ (٧٥)
There is no invisible thing in the heaven and the earth but it is in a manifest Book. (75)
إِنَّ هَـٰذَا ٱلْقُرْءَانَ يَقُصُّ عَلَىٰ بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ أَكْثَرَ ٱلَّذِى هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٧٦)
This Quran recounts for the Children of Israel most of what they differ about, (76)
وَإِنَّهُۥ لَهُدًى وَرَحْمَةٌ لِّلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٧)
and it is indeed a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (77)
إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُم بِحُكْمِهِۦ ۚ وَهُوَ ٱلْعَزِيزُ ٱلْعَلِيمُ (٧٨)
Your Lord will decide between them by His judgement, and He is the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (78)
فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ ۖ إِنَّكَ عَلَى ٱلْحَقِّ ٱلْمُبِينِ (٧٩)
So put your trust in Allah, for you indeed stand on the manifest truth. (79)
إِنَّكَ لَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلْمَوْتَىٰ وَلَا تُسْمِعُ ٱلصُّمَّ ٱلدُّعَآءَ إِذَا وَلَّوْا۟ مُدْبِرِينَ (٨٠)
You cannot make the dead hear, nor can you make the deaf listen to your call when they turn their backs, (80)
وَمَآ أَنتَ بِهَـٰدِى ٱلْعُمْىِ عَن ضَلَـٰلَتِهِمْ ۖ إِن تُسْمِعُ إِلَّا مَن يُؤْمِنُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُم مُّسْلِمُونَ (٨١)
nor can you lead the blind out of their error. You can make only those hear you who believe in Our signs and have submitted. (81)

(Commentary)

This is a transition from the stories He, the Glorified, has narrated—which are exemplars of His established sunnah (السنة) — the habitual way of Allah’s action — prevailing in the human species regarding His guiding them and showing them the path of their felicity in life, His honoring those among them who were guided to the straight path (الصراط المستقيم) — the direct and unerring way to Allah — through election (الاصطفاء) and magnificent favors, and His seizing those who associated partners with Him, turned away from His remembrance, and plotted against Him with the punishment of extirpation and painful exemplary chastisement.

The transition moves toward His praise and peace upon His elect servants, and the affirmation that He alone is worthy of servitude (العبودية) — the state of total submission and devotion to Allah — to the exclusion of others whom they associate with Him. Then, the discourse continues regarding monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — the proof of the Return (المعاد) — the restoration of life for judgment — and related matters of diverse true cognitions (المعارف). Thus, the context of the verses of this Surah is similar to the context found in Surah Maryam, as previously discussed.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ — Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah, and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen.’ Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]? (27:59). When He narrated the stories of the prophets and their nations—containing the explanation of His established sunnah prevailing in past nations, what He did for the believers among them in terms of election and increased favor (as with the prophets among them), and what He did to the disbelievers in terms of punishment and destruction—and since He has done nothing but beautiful good, nor has His sunnah run except according to profound wisdom (الحكمة البالغة), He transitioned from there to commanding His Prophet to praise Him, to laud Him, and to send peace upon the elect among His servants, affirming that He, the Exalted, is the one designated for worship (العبادة).

It is a transition from stories to praise, salutation, and monotheism; it is not a logical deduction (استنتاج), even if it functions as one. Otherwise, it would have been said: "So say, 'All praise belongs to Allah...'" or "So is Allah better...".

His saying: قُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ (Say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah’) is a command to praise Him, and it involves returning every praise to Him, the Exalted. This is because the preceding verses established that the ultimate return of all creation (خلق) and governance (تدبير) is to Him; He is the Bestower of every good by His wisdom and the Doer of every beautiful act by His power.

His saying: وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى (and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen) is conjoined to what precedes it as part of the commanded speech. In the salutation (التسليم) upon those elect servants, there is a negation of all aspects of resistance and opposition within the soul of the one who offers peace toward the divine guidance (الهداية الإلهية) they possess and its beautiful effects—as necessitated by the meaning of peace (السلام). Thus, in the command to offer peace, there is an implicit command to prepare oneself to accept the guidance and its effects that they possess. In a sense, it carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: أُولئِكَ الَّذِينَ هَدَى اللهُ فَبِهُداهُمُ اقْتَدِهْ — They are the ones whom Allah has guided. So follow their guidance. (6:90). So understand this.

His saying: آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe [to Him]?) is part of the completion of the address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the interrogation is for the purpose of affirmation (التقرير). The sum of the intended meaning is that if all praise is for Allah and He is the one who chooses His elect servants, then He is better than their deities whom they worship, who possess neither creation nor governance for which they could be praised, nor do they hold any good in their hands to bestow upon their worshippers.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ خَلَقَ السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضَ وَأَنْزَلَ لَكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً — Is He who created the heavens and the earth, and sends down for you water from the sky... (27:60) to the end of the verse. The word "gardens" (الحدائق) is the plural of hadiqah (حديقة), which is a garden that is bounded and enclosed by walls. "Of delightful beauty" (ذات بهجة) is an attribute of the gardens. It is stated in Majma‘ al-Bayan: "Of delightful beauty (ذات بهجة) means having a beautiful appearance that delights whoever sees it. He did not say dhawat (ذوات) [plural] because He intended the femininity of the collective group (الجماعة); had He intended the femininity of the individual entities (الأعيان), He would have said dhawat." End quote.

The word "or" (أَمْ) in the verse is "disjunctive" (منقطعة), providing the sense of "rather" (الإضراب). The word "He who" (مَنْ) is a subject (مبتدأ) whose predicate (خبر) is omitted, as is the other side of the alternative. The interrogation is for affirmation and to compel them to acknowledge the truth. The estimation (التقدير), based on what the context indicates, is: "Rather, is He who created the heavens and the earth... better, or that which they associate?" The matter follows this pattern in the four subsequent verses.

The meaning of the verse is: Rather, is He who created the heavens and the earth and sent down for you—meaning for your benefit—from the sky (السماء), which is the direction of height, water (ماء), which is rain, by which We caused to grow gardens of delightful beauty and radiance, the trees of which you were not—meaning you do not possess the power and it is not within your capacity—to cause to grow. Is there another god (إله) with Allah, the Glorified? This is a rhetorical question of denial (إنكار) and rebuke (توبيخ).

In the verse, there is a shift (التفات) from the third person (الغيبة) to the second person (الخطاب) regarding the polytheists. The subtle point (النكتة) in this is to intensify the rebuke by changing absence into presence. The station of the verses preceding this one was the station of a speaker addressing one of his close associates in the presence of his rebellious servants who have turned away from his servitude, venting his complaint to him while they listen. Once the proof (الحجة) was completed and the evidence (البينة) established—as in His saying: آللهُ خَيْرٌ أَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (Is Allah better, or the partners they ascribe?)—grief and regret were stirred. He then turned toward them after having turned away, beginning to compel them to acknowledge the truth by mentioning sign after sign, denying their polytheism and rebuking them for it, for their turning away from Him to others, for the lack of knowledge of most of them, and their lack of remembrance, despite His transcendence above their shirk and their lack of proof for what they claim.

His saying: بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ — Rather, they are a people who deviate. (27:60). That is, they deviate from the truth to falsehood and from Allah, the Glorified, to others. It is also said: they equate others with Allah and treat them as equals.

In this sentence, there is a shift from the second person to the third person regarding the polytheists, returning to the address of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. The "rather" (الإضراب) here is to explain that there is no utility in continuing to try to bring them to the truth, for they are deviators from it.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ جَعَلَ الْأَرْضَ قَراراً — Is He who made the earth an abode... (27:61) to the end of the verse. "Abode" (القرار) is an infinitive used in the sense of the active participle, meaning "stable" or "settled." "Fissures" (الخلال) is the plural of khalal, which is the gap between two things. "Firm mountains" (الرواسي) is the plural of rasiyah, meaning "fixed," referring to the stable mountains. "Barrier" (الحاجز) is the preventer that intervenes between two things.

The meaning is: Rather, is He who made the earth stable so that it does not sway with you, and made rivers in the gaps within its interior, and made firm mountains for it, and placed between the two seas a barrier preventing them from mixing and blending—is He better, or that which they associate? The discussion regarding His saying: أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (Is there a god besides Allah? Rather, most of them do not know) is the same as the discussion regarding its counterpart in the previous verse.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ وَيَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ قَلِيلاً ما تَذَكَّرُونَ — Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him, and removes the distress, and makes you successors on the earth? Is there a god besides Allah? Little is the heed you take. (27:62). What is meant by answering the distressed one when he calls Him is the responding to the supplication of the supplicants and the fulfilling of their needs. The description of "distress" (الاضطرار) was taken so that the reality of supplication (الدعاء) and petition (المسألة) is realized from the supplicant. For as long as a human being has not fallen into the straits of distress and remains in a state of sufficiency regarding the objective, the request does not become pure and sincere; this is evident.

Then He restricted it with His saying: إِذا دَعاهُ (when he calls Him) to indicate that the one called must be Allah, the Glorified. This only occurs when the supplicant is cut off from all apparent causes (الأسباب الظاهرية) and his heart becomes attached to his Lord alone. As for the one whose heart is attached to apparent causes only, or to a combination of his Lord and those causes, he is not truly calling upon his Lord; he is calling upon another.

So, if the supplication is sincere and the one called is his Lord alone, then He, the Exalted, answers him and removes the evil (السوء) that compelled him to the petition, as He, the Exalted, said: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ — Call Me, I will answer you. (40:60). He did not stipulate for the response anything except that there be a true supplication and that this supplication be directed to Him alone. He also said: وَإِذا سَأَلَكَ عِبادِي عَنِّي فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ — When My servants ask you about Me, [tell them that] I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me. (2:186). We have detailed the meaning of supplication (الدعاء) in the second volume of this book under that verse.

From the preceding explanation, the invalidity of the claim made by some becomes clear—that the definite article (ال) in "the distressed" (الْمُضْطَرَّ) is for the genus (الجنس) rather than for exhaustion/universality (الاستغراق), arguing that many distressed people call out but are not answered, so the meaning is the answering of the distressed one's call in some cases, not all.

The reason for its invalidity is that verses such as: ادْعُونِي أَسْتَجِبْ لَكُمْ (Call Me, I will answer you) and His saying: فَإِنِّي قَرِيبٌ أُجِيبُ دَعْوَةَ الدَّاعِ إِذا دَعانِ (I am indeed nearmost. I answer the supplicant’s call when he calls Me) refuse the failure of the response to follow the supplication. As for the claim that "many a distressed person calls but is not answered," it is not accepted if it is a true supplication to Allah, the Glorified, alone, as previously explained.

Furthermore, there are many verses indicating that a human being, upon distress—such as when riding a ship—turns toward his Lord and calls Him with sincerity (الإخلاص), and is answered, such as His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا مَسَّ الْإِنْسانَ الضُّرُّ دَعانا لِجَنْبِهِ أَوْ قاعِداً أَوْ قائِماً — When distress befalls man, he supplicates Us, [lying] on his side, or sitting, or standing... (10:12), and His saying: حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ (until, when you are in the ships...) until His saying: وَظَنُّوا أَنَّهُمْ أُحِيطَ بِهِمْ دَعَوُا اللهَ مُخْلِصِينَ لَهُ الدِّينَ — ...and they think they are besieged, they invoke Allah, putting exclusive faith in Him... (10:22). How can the soul be conceived as attaching itself through its instinctive primordial nature (الفطرة) to a matter in which it has no confidence? The judgment of the primordial nature in this regard is exactly like its judgment when perceiving its existential need for the one who brings it into existence and governs its affairs: that there is a reality that fulfills its need, and that is Allah, the Glorified.

If you say: We often seek recourse (نتوسل) for our needs through apparent causes whose actual effectiveness in removing our need we are not certain of, and we only attach ourselves to them in the hope that they might benefit us if they do.

I say: This is an intellectual recourse (توسل فكري) whose origin is greed and hope, and it is different from the instinctive primordial recourse (التوسل الغريزي الفطري). Yes, within it is a type of instinctive turning, which is the seeking of a cause through the absolute concept of "cause," and the absolute cause never fails. So understand.

The invalidity of the claim that "the distressed one when he calls Him" refers to the sinner when he seeks forgiveness—for Allah forgives him, and that is His answering—also becomes clear.

The problem with this is that the issue of universality (الاستغراق) remains as it is: not every seeking of forgiveness is followed by forgiveness, nor is every seeker of forgiveness forgiven. Furthermore, there is no evidence to restrict the absolute term "distressed" to the disobedient sinner.

Some have mentioned: The universality remains, but the response should be restricted by [Allah's] will (المشية), as occurred in His saying, the Exalted: فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ — ...then He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes... (6:41).

The problem with this is that the verse [6:41] occurs in a context that makes it unsuitable for restricting the response in the verse of the distressed. That context is His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ أَرَأَيْتَكُمْ إِنْ أَتاكُمْ عَذابُ اللهِ أَوْ أَتَتْكُمُ السَّاعَةُ أَغَيْرَ اللهِ تَدْعُونَ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ بَلْ إِيَّاهُ تَدْعُونَ فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ — Say, ‘Tell me, should Allah’s punishment come to you, or should the Hour come to you, will you supplicate anyone other than Allah, should you be truthful? Rather Him you will supplicate, and He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes... (6:40-41). The Hour (الساعة) is of the decreed destiny (القضاء المحتوم) for which no true request for removal can be attached. As for the divine punishment, if the request for its removal is through true repentance and faith, then Allah removes it, as He removed it from the people of Yunus. If it is not so, but rather a ruse to escape it, then it is not removed because it is not a true request, but rather a plot in the form of a request, as Allah narrated regarding Pharaoh when drowning overtook him: قالَ آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائيلَ وَأَنَا مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ — ...he said, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit [to Him].’ [He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you were disobedient before and were among the agents of corruption?’ (10:90-91). He also narrated regarding other peoples seized by punishment: قالُوا يا وَيْلَنا إِنَّا كُنَّا ظالِمِينَ فَما زالَتْ تِلْكَ دَعْواهُمْ حَتَّى جَعَلْناهُمْ حَصِيداً خامِدِينَ — They said, ‘Woe to us! We were indeed wrongdoers.’ That remained their cry until We made them like a mown field, extinct. (21:14-15).

In short, the instance of His saying: فَيَكْشِفُ ما تَدْعُونَ إِلَيْهِ إِنْ شاءَ (then He will remove that for which you supplicated Him, if He wishes), since it was something where the request could be true or not true, it was necessary to restrict the removal and the response therein by the will; so Allah removes it from them if He wishes—and that is in the instance of a true request and faith—and He does not remove it if He does not wish. This is different from the instance of the verse of the distressed and other verses of answering the call, which involve the reality of supplication to Allah, the Glorified, alone.

His saying: وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ (and makes you successors on the earth). What the context provides is that the intended meaning of succession (الخلافة) is the earthly succession that Allah has granted to the human being, whereby he disposes of the earth and the creation within it as he wishes, as He, the Exalted, said: وَإِذْ قالَ رَبُّكَ لِلْمَلائِكَةِ إِنِّي جاعِلٌ فِي الْأَرْضِ خَلِيفَةً — When your Lord said to the angels, ‘Indeed I am going to set a viceroy on the earth...’ (2:30).

This is because his disposals (تصرفات) which he carries out in the earth and what is in it by virtue of his succession are matters linked to his life and related to his livelihood. The evil (السوء) that brings him to the point of distress and for which he asks Allah's removal is inevitably something that prevents him from disposal or some disposal therein, and closes upon him the door of life, survival, and what pertains to them, or some of those doors. Thus, in the removal of evil from him, his succession is completed.

This meaning becomes even clearer if the supplication and petition in His saying: إِذا دَعاهُ (when he calls Him) is taken to be more general than verbal supplication, as is apparent from His saying, the Exalted: وَآتاكُمْ مِنْ كُلِّ ما سَأَلْتُمُوهُ وَإِنْ تَعُدُّوا نِعْمَتَ اللهِ لا تُحْصُوها — He gave you all that you asked Him. If you enumerate Allah’s blessings, you will not be able to count them. (14:34), and His saying: يَسْئَلُهُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ — Everyone in the heavens and the earth asks Him. (55:29). For on this basis, all that the human being has been given and provided with in terms of disposals are instances of the removal of evil from the distressed and needy one following his supplication. Thus, making him a successor follows the answering of his supplication and the removal of the evil that distressed him.

It is said: The meaning is "and makes you successors of the nations before you on the earth," inhabiting their dwellings and disposing of them after them. This, however, is less suitable to the context than the meaning we have presented.

It is also said: The meaning is "and makes you successors of the disbelievers by inheriting their lands and obeying Allah after their polytheism and obstinacy." The problem with this is that the address in the verse, like the other five verses before it, is to the disbelievers, not the believers, as this view would require.

His saying: قَلِيلاً ما تَذَكَّرُونَ (Little is the heed you take) is a rebuking address to the disbelievers. It is also read yadhakkarun (يذكرون) with a ya for the third person, which is preferable as it matches what is at the end of the other five verses, such as His saying: بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ (Rather, they are a people who deviate) and بَلْ أَكْثَرُهُمْ لا يَعْلَمُونَ (Rather, most of them do not know), for the address in all of them is to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله by way of shift (التفات), as previously explained.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ يَهْدِيكُمْ فِي ظُلُماتِ الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ وَمَنْ يُرْسِلُ الرِّياحَ بُشْراً بَيْنَ يَدَيْ رَحْمَتِهِ — Is He who guides you in the darkness of land and sea, and who sends the winds as heralds of His mercy? (27:63) etc. What is meant by the darkness of land and sea is the darkness of the nights in the land and sea; this involves a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي). What is meant by sending the winds as heralds is sending them as bringers of glad tidings of rain shortly before its descent, and "mercy" (الرحمة) is the rain. The rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ يَبْدَؤُا الْخَلْقَ ثُمَّ يُعِيدُهُ وَمَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ — Is He who originates the creation then restores it, and who provides for you from the sky and the earth? (27:64) etc. Originating the creation (بدء الخلق) is bringing it into existence for the first time, and restoring it (إعادته) is returning it to Him through the Resurrection (البعث). The silencing (تبكيت) of the polytheists through the beginning and the restoration—despite their denial of the Resurrection, as He will mention in His saying: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا (And the faithless say...) etc.—is based on the fact that the Return is established by definitive proofs in His speech. Thus, it was taken as an accepted premise, and then their denial or doubt regarding it was addressed in the following verses.

It is said: What is meant by originating the creation then restoring it is the bringing into existence of an individual of a species, then its destruction, and the bringing into existence of its like after it. In short, the bringing into existence of like after like. Thus, the objection that the polytheists deny the Return, so how can it be used as an argument against them, is avoided. This, however, is far from the apparent meaning of the verse.

What the verse contains of the subtle Quranic truths indicates that there is no absolute annihilation (بطلان) in existence; rather, what Allah, the Exalted, brought into existence through the beginning shall return to Him through the restoration. What we witness of destruction therein is merely our loss of it after having found it.

As for what the theologians (المتكلمون) have agreed upon regarding the impossibility of restoring the non-existent (إعادة المعدوم) in some existents like accidents (الأعراض), and their disagreement regarding the permissibility of restoring others like substances (الجواهر), it has no connection to the issue of the Resurrection as established by the verse. For the Resurrection is not of the nature of restoring the non-existent such that it would be impossible if its restoration were impossible; rather, the Resurrection is the return of the creation and its coming back—which is a creation without annihilation—to its Lord who originated it.

His saying: وَمَنْ يَرْزُقُكُمْ مِنَ السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ (and who provides for you from the sky and the earth) is an allusion to what occurs of His governance of their affairs between the beginning and the return, which is their provision through celestial causes like rains and their factors, and terrestrial causes like all that the human being feeds upon from the earth.

His saying: قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — Say, ‘Produce your proof, if you are truthful.’ (27:64). When He, the Glorified, mentioned sections encompassing the whole of creation and governance, while alluding to the connection of governance to one another and the connection of all to creation—whereby creation and governance became a single matter attributed to Him and subsisting through Him, the Exalted—He thereby established that He, the Exalted, is the Lord of everything alone, without partner. The necessary consequence of this was the invalidation of the divinity (ألوهية) of the deities they call upon besides Allah.

This is because divinity—which is the entitlement to worship—follows Lordship (الربوبية), which is governance (تدبير) arising from ownership (ملك). Worship, as they practice it, is either to be gratitude for a blessing or protection from a calamity; in either case, it is linked to governance, which is among the affairs of Lordship.

Since the invalidation of the divinity of deities besides Allah was the purpose of the sections presented in these verses—as indicated by His saying after presenting each section: أَإِلهٌ مَعَ اللهِ (Is there a god besides Allah?)—He commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله with His saying: قُلْ هاتُوا بُرْهانَكُمْ (Say, ‘Produce your proof’) to demand from them the proof for what they claim regarding the divinity of their deities, so that it may become clear through their inability that they are making arbitrary claims. For if they were to argue for their divinity with anything, they would have to attribute to them some part of the governance of the world, whereas all creation and governance belong to Him, the Exalted, alone.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ — Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah, and they do not know when they will be resurrected.’ (27:65). When He commanded him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, after invalidating the divinity of their deities by attributing creation and governance to Him, the Exalted, alone, to demand proof from them for what they claim, He commanded him a second time to confront them with another proof for the invalidation of the divinity of their deities: their lack of knowledge of the Unseen (الغيب) — that which lies beyond sensory perception and empirical knowledge — and their lack of awareness of the Hour and when they will be resurrected. This is while no one among those in the heavens and the earth—including their deities who are angels, jinn, and saintly humans—knows the Unseen, nor do they know when they will be resurrected. Had they been gods possessing the governance of creation—and among governance is the reward on the Day of Resurrection—they would have known the Hour.

It has become clear through this explanation that His saying: لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ (No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah) is an independent proof for the invalidation of the divinity of their deities and the exclusivity of divinity to Him, the Exalted, alone. His saying: وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (and they do not know when they will be resurrected) is the conjoining of the clearest and most important instances of the Unseen to it in terms of knowledge regarding the matter of governance.

It has also become clear that the two plural pronouns in وَما يَشْعُرُونَ أَيَّانَ يُبْعَثُونَ (and they do not know when they will be resurrected) refer to "those in the heavens and the earth," for the explanation would not be complete without it.

As for the claim of some that the pronoun refers to the polytheists—even if the lack of awareness of what was mentioned is general—to avoid a disconnect (تفكيك) between it and the subsequent pronouns which certainly refer to them, it is problematic. This is because it contradicts the purpose for which the noble verse was brought, as we have previously indicated. A disconnect between pronouns when a contextual clue (قرينة) exists is not problematic.

His saying, the Exalted: بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها بَلْ هُمْ مِنْها عَمُونَ — Do they have a full knowledge of the Hereafter? Rather, they are in doubt about it. Rather, they are blind to it. (27:66). The word ادارك (iddaraka) is originally tadāraka. Tadāruk is the following of parts of a thing one after another until they are exhausted and nothing remains of them. The meaning of "their knowledge has been exhausted (تدارك) regarding the Hereafter" is that they spent what knowledge they had on other things until their knowledge was depleted, and nothing remained of it by which they could perceive the matter of the Hereafter, similar to His saying, the Exalted: فَأَعْرِضْ عَنْ مَنْ تَوَلَّى عَنْ ذِكْرِنا وَلَمْ يُرِدْ إِلَّا الْحياةَ الدُّنْيا ذلِكَ مَبْلَغُهُمْ مِنَ الْعِلْمِ — So avoid those who turn away from Our remembrance and desire nothing but the life of the world. That is the ultimate reach of their knowledge. (53:29-30). "Blind" (عَمُونَ) is the plural of ‘amin.

When His, the Exalted, argumentation reached the mention of the lack of awareness of anyone other than Him, the Exalted, regarding the time of the Resurrection and the silencing of the polytheists thereby, He returned to His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and reminded him that they are excluded from being addressed by that. For they have no news of anything regarding the matters of the Hereafter, let alone the time of the standing of the Hour. This is because they spent what knowledge they had on the aspects of the life of this world; thus, they are in absolute ignorance regarding the matters of the Hereafter. Rather, they are in doubt (شك) about the Hereafter, wavering in its matter, as appears from their arguments for its negation based on improbability. Rather, they are blind (عمون) to it; Allah has blinded their hearts from testifying to it and believing in its existence.

It has become clear through this explanation that the repetition of the word of "rather" (الإضراب) is to explain the levels of deprivation from knowledge of the Hereafter, and that they are at the highest of them. His saying: بَلِ ادَّارَكَ عِلْمُهُمْ فِي الْآخِرَةِ (Do they have a full knowledge of the Hereafter?) means they have no knowledge of it, as if it has never struck their ears. His saying: بَلْ هُمْ فِي شَكٍّ مِنْها (Rather, they are in doubt about it) means its news has struck their ears and entered their hearts, but they wavered and did not believe in it. His saying: بَلْ هُمْ مِنْها عَمُونَ (Rather, they are blind to it) means they did not merely cease to believe in it of their own accord and by their own choice; rather, Allah, the Glorified, blinded the eyes of their hearts, so they became blind. Thus, it is far-fetched that they should perceive anything of its matter.

It is said: What is meant by the "attainment" (تدارك) of their knowledge is its perfection and its reaching the level of certainty due to the perfection of the proofs indicating the truth of the Resurrection, and the sentence is intended as sarcasm (تهكم). The problem with this is that it does not suit the subsequent "rather" involving doubt and blindness.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقالَ الَّذِينَ كَفَرُوا أَإِذا كُنَّا تُراباً وَآباؤُنا أَإِنَّا لَمُخْرَجُونَ — The faithless say, ‘What! When we and our fathers have become dust, shall we really be brought forth [from the grave]? (27:67) to His saying: الْأَوَّلِينَ (of the ancients). This is a narration of an argument from them to negate the Resurrection based on improbability. That is: how is it possible that we should be brought forth from the earth as complete human beings as we are today, when we have died and become dust, both we and our fathers likewise?

His saying: لَقَدْ وُعِدْنا هذا نَحْنُ وَآباؤُنا مِنْ قَبْلُ — We and our fathers were certainly promised this before... (27:68) is another argument from them based on improbability. That is: we were certainly promised this—meaning the Resurrection after death—we and our fathers, before this Prophet promised us this. Those who promised before were the past prophets. So it is an ancient promise that we have continued to be promised; had it been a true report and a real promise, it would have occurred by this day. Since it has not occurred, it is among the myths (خرافات) fabricated by the ancients, who were fond of fabricating delusions and myths and listening to them.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ سِيرُوا فِي الْأَرْضِ فَانْظُرُوا كَيْفَ كانَ عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ — Say, ‘Travel over the land and observe how was the fate of the guilty.’ (27:69). This is a warning and a frightening of them for their denial of the prophets' promise of the Resurrection, by commanding them to travel through the land and observe what was the end of the criminals (المجرمين) who denied the prophets who warned them of the Resurrection. For in observing the end of their affair, as indicated by their ruined dwellings and empty lands, there is sufficiency for those who take heed among the possessors of insight.
