In expressing the deniers as "criminals," there is a kindness toward the believers in their avoidance of crimes. So it is said.

It is possible to establish the verse as a proof indicating the Return. The approximation of this is that the end of the criminals' affair in the punishment of extirpation is proof that crime and injustice are of a nature that warrants being taken to task, and that an action—whether it be an act of excellence or a crime—is preserved for its doer and he will be held accountable for it. Since the entirety of this accounting and reward—especially for righteous deeds (الصالحات)—does not occur in this world, it must inevitably be in another world, which is the Abode of the Hereafter.

Thus, the verse would be in the sense of His saying, the Exalted: أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الَّذِينَ آمَنُوا وَعَمِلُوا الصَّالِحاتِ كَالْمُفْسِدِينَ فِي الْأَرْضِ أَمْ نَجْعَلُ الْمُتَّقِينَ كَالْفُجَّارِ — Shall We treat those who have faith and do righteous deeds like those who cause corruption on the earth? Shall We treat the God-conscious like the vicious? (38:28). This interpretation is supported by His saying: عاقِبَةُ الْمُجْرِمِينَ (the fate of the guilty). Had the intention been to threaten the deniers of the messengers and frighten them, it would have been more suitable to say: "the fate of the deniers," as previously indicated.

His saying, the Exalted: وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ وَلا تَكُنْ فِي ضَيْقٍ مِمَّا يَمْكُرُونَ — Do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting. (27:70). That is: let not their persistence in disbelief and denial grieve you, and let not your breast be straitened by their plotting to invalidate your call and their barring people from the way of Allah. For they are under the eye of Allah and they cannot frustrate Him, and He will soon requite them for their deeds.

The verse is intended to comfort the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. His saying: وَلا تَكُنْ فِي ضَيْقٍ (and do not be upset) etc., is conjoined to what precedes it as an explanatory conjunction.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَقُولُونَ مَتى هذَا الْوَعْدُ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ صادِقِينَ — They say, ‘When will this promise be fulfilled, if you are truthful?’ (27:71). It appears that what is meant by the promise is the promise of the punishment of requital, more general than this world and the Hereafter. The context supports this, and the rest is clear.

His saying, the Exalted: قُلْ عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُمْ بَعْضُ الَّذِي تَسْتَعْجِلُونَ — Say, ‘It may be that some of what you seek to hasten has already come close behind you.’ (27:72). They said: The lam in رَدِفَ لَكُمْ (radifa lakum) is extra for emphasis, like the ba in His saying: وَلا تُلْقُوا بِأَيْدِيكُمْ إِلَى التَّهْلُكَةِ — ...and do not cast yourselves with your own hands into destruction... (2:195). The meaning is: it has followed you and caught up with you. It is also said that radifa implies the meaning of a verb that takes its object with a lam.

What is meant by "some of what they seek to hasten" is the punishment of this world before the punishment of the Hereafter. For they were seeking to hasten the fulfillment of what Allah promised them of the decisive judgment, which is inseparable from their punishment. Their punishment in this world is some of the punishment they seek to hasten by demanding the fulfillment of the promise. Perhaps the verse refers to the punishment of the Day of Badr, as has been said.

They said: "Perhaps" (‘asa) and "maybe" (la‘alla) from Allah, the Exalted, are mandatory (واجب), because the reality of hope (الترجي) is based on ignorance, and that is not permissible for Him, the Exalted. So the meaning of His saying: عَسى أَنْ يَكُونَ رَدِفَ لَكُمْ is: the punishment will surely follow you and come to you as a certainty.

The problem with this is that the meaning of hope, wishing, and the like, just as it is permissible to subsist in the soul of the speaker, it is permissible to subsist in the context, or in the listener, or others. In His speech, the Exalted, it subsists in someone other than the speaker, such as the context and so on. Since the answer in the verse is returned to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, the hope indicated by the word ‘asa subsists in his noble soul. The meaning is: Say, I hope that the punishment has followed close behind you.

In the Tafsir of Abu al-Su‘ud: "‘Asa, la‘alla, and sawfa in the promises of kings are equivalent to certainty. They only use them to display dignity and to signal that a hint from the likes of them is like an explicit statement from others. The promise and threat of Allah, the Exalted, follow this course." End quote. This is a sound view.

The meaning of the verse is: Say to these questioners about the time of the promise: I hope that some of the promise you seek to hasten has followed you, which is the punishment of this world that brings you near to the punishment of the Hereafter and leads you to it. In the expression رَدِفَ لَكُمْ (has come close behind you), there is an allusion to its proximity.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَذُو فَضْلٍ عَلَى النَّاسِ وَلَكِنَّ أَكْثَرَهُمْ لا يَشْكُرُونَ — Indeed your Lord is gracious to mankind, but most of them do not give thanks. (27:73). The meaning of the verse in itself is clear. Its occurrence in the context of threat and frightening indicates that His, the Exalted, delaying the punishment from them despite their deserving it is only a grace (فضل) from Him upon them, for which they must thank Him, but they do not thank Him and instead ask for its hastening.

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِنَّ رَبَّكَ لَيَعْلَمُ ما تُكِنُّ صُدُورُهُمْ وَما يُعْلِنُونَ — Indeed your Lord knows what their breasts conceal and what they disclose. (27:74). That is: the delay of the punishment is not due to ignorance on His part, the Exalted, regarding their state and what they deserve of disbelief and denial; for He knows what their breasts hide and conceal, and what they manifest.

Then He emphasized this by stating that every hidden thing (غائبة)—which is that which is of a nature to be absent and hidden in any part of the world—is written and preserved with Him, the Exalted. This is His saying: وَما مِنْ غائِبَةٍ فِي السَّماءِ وَالْأَرْضِ إِلَّا فِي كِتابٍ مُبِينٍ — There is no invisible thing in the heaven and the earth but it is in a manifest Book. (27:75).

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ — Indeed this Quran relates to the Children of Israel... (27:76) to His saying: الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ — ...the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (27:78). This is a comfort for the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, a preparation for what He will mention regarding the truth of his call, and a strengthening of the faith of the believers in him. In this way, it connects to His saying earlier: وَلا تَحْزَنْ عَلَيْهِمْ (Do not grieve for them) etc., which intimates the truth of his call.

His saying: إِنَّ هذَا الْقُرْآنَ يَقُصُّ عَلى بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ أَكْثَرَ الَّذِي هُمْ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ — Indeed this Quran relates to the Children of Israel most of what they differ about. (27:76) refers to what the Quran relates of the stories of the prophets and explains the truth in what they differed about regarding their affairs—among which is the matter of the Messiah (peace be upon him)—and explains the truth in what they differed about regarding cognitions and laws.

His saying: وَإِنَّهُ لَهُدىً وَرَحمةٌ لِلْمُؤْمِنِينَ — and it is indeed a guidance and mercy for the faithful. (27:77) indicates that it guides the believers through what it relates to the Children of Israel toward the truth, and that it is a mercy for them by which their hearts find peace and faith is established in their souls thereby.

His saying: إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ بِحُكْمِهِ وَهُوَ الْعَزِيزُ الْعَلِيمُ — Indeed your Lord will judge between them by His judgment, and He is the All-mighty, the All-knowing. (27:78) is an allusion to the fact that the judgment between them belongs to Allah; He is your Lord, the All-mighty (العزيز) who is not overcome in His affair, the All-knowing (العليم) who is not ignorant and does not err in His judgment. He is the Judge between them by His judgment. So let the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله be content with his Lord, the All-mighty, the All-knowing, as a Judge and Arbiter, and let him return the matter to Him, as he should do likewise regarding the polytheists: "do not grieve for them, and do not be upset by their plotting."

His saying, the Exalted: فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّكَ عَلَى الْحَقِّ الْمُبِينِ — So put your trust in Allah; indeed you stand on the manifest truth. (27:79). This is a derivation from the sum of what he was commanded to do in the face of the disbelievers' disbelief and the Children of Israel's differences. That is: the affair of all of them belongs to Allah, not to you; so take Him as a trustee (وكيلاً), for He is sufficient for you, and do not fear anything, for you are in the security of the truth.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى — You cannot make the dead hear... (27:80) to His saying: فَهُمْ مُسْلِمُونَ — ...so they submit. (27:81). This is a justification for the command to trust [in Allah]. That is: We only commanded you to trust in Allah regarding the matter of their faith and disbelief because they are dead, and it is not within your power to make the dead hear your call. They are deaf and do not hear, and blind and lost; you are not able to make the deaf hear when they turn their backs—perhaps He restricted the inability to make the deaf hear with His saying: إِذا وَلَّوْا مُدْبِرِينَ (when they turn their backs) because if they were not turning away, it might be possible to make them understand through some kind of sign—nor can you guide the blind out of their error. Rather, the one you are able to make hear is he who believes in Our signs (آياتنا) indicating Us, and you guide them, for due to their submission to those true proofs, they are submissive (مسلمون) to Us, testifying to what they indicate.

Through this explanation, it has become clear: First: That what is meant by "making hear" (الإسماع) is guidance (الهداية). Second: That what is meant by "signs" (الآيات) are the proofs indicating monotheism and the true cognitions that follow it. Third: That whoever perceives the true proofs from the signs of the horizons and the souls with a sound intellect, then submits to them through faith and compliance, is not among the dead, nor among those whose hearing and sight Allah has sealed.

(Narrative Discussion)

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَسَلامٌ عَلى عِبادِهِ الَّذِينَ اصْطَفى (and peace be to His servants whom He has chosen), he said: "They are the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام)."

I say: It was also narrated in Jam‘ al-Jawami‘ from them (peace be upon them) as a disconnected (مرسلاً) and unnamed (مضمراً) report. You have already learned from the preceding explanation under the verse that what the context provides is that the intended meaning—according to the instance of the verse—is the prophets who were blessed with the favor of election, and Allah has narrated the stories of a group of them. Thus, his saying (peace be upon him)—if the narration is authentic—"They are the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام)" is of the nature of "flow and application" (الجري والانطباق) — the principle that Quranic verses apply to new instances beyond their original context.

Similar to it is what was narrated in al-Durr al-Manthur from several authors of books, from Ibn ‘Abbas regarding the verse: "They are the companions of Muhammad." This—if the narration is authentic—is an instance of "flow and application" on his part.

From this, the nature of what was also narrated from ‘Abd ibn Humayd and Ibn Jarir from Sufyan al-Thawri regarding the verse becomes clear: "It was revealed specifically concerning the companions of Muhammad." There is neither a specific revelation (نزول) nor exclusivity (اختصاص).

In the Tafsir of al-Qummi, also regarding His saying, the Exalted: بَلْ هُمْ قَوْمٌ يَعْدِلُونَ (Rather, they are a people who deviate), he said: "From the truth."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ (Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him), it is narrated: My father narrated to me from al-Hasan ibn ‘Ali ibn Faddal from Salih ibn ‘Uqbah from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "It was revealed concerning the Riser (القائم) from the Progeny of Muhammad (عليه‌السلام). He, by Allah, is the distressed one when he prays two units of prayer at the Station [of Ibrahim] and calls upon Allah (عز وجل); so He answers him, removes the evil, and makes him a successor on the earth."

I say: The narration is also of the nature of "flow," while the verse is general.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, al-Tabarani narrated from Sa‘d ibn Junadah, who said: The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله said: "Whoever separates from the community (الجماعة) shall be in the Fire on his face—because Allah, the Exalted, says: أَمَّنْ يُجِيبُ الْمُضْطَرَّ إِذا دَعاهُ ـ وَيَكْشِفُ السُّوءَ وَيَجْعَلُكُمْ خُلَفاءَ الْأَرْضِ (Is He who answers the distressed one when he calls Him, and removes the distress, and makes you successors on the earth?). For succession (الخلافة) is from Allah (عز وجل); if it is good, He lets it proceed, and if it is evil, he is taken to task for it. You must adhere to obedience in what Allah has commanded."

I say: The narration is not without issues. It has already been mentioned that the intended meaning of succession in the verse—as witnessed by the context—is the earthly succession decreed for every human being, which is the authority over what is on the earth through various types of disposal, not succession in the sense of government over the nation by managing the wheel of their society.

Even disregarding that, the text of the narration is not free from internal contradiction. If the meaning of succession being "from Allah" is that his authority over the people is by a decree from Allah—in other words, its ontological attribution (الانتساب التكويني) to Allah, the Glorified, as mentioned regarding the kingdom of Nimrod in His saying: أَنْ آتاهُ اللهُ الْمُلْكَ — ...because Allah had given him the kingdom... (2:258), and His saying narrating from Pharaoh: أَلَيْسَ لِي مُلْكُ مِصْرَ — ...‘Does not the kingdom of Egypt belong to me...’ (43:51)—then it is clear that succession in this sense does not necessitate the obligation of obedience and the prohibition of opposition. Otherwise, it would be a violation of the foundation of the religious call and an obligation to obey the likes of Nimrod and Pharaoh, of which there are many examples. If the meaning is the religious conventional designation (الجعل الوضعي الديني)—in other words, its legislative attribution (الانتساب التشريعي) to Allah, the Exalted—and then obedience to him became obligatory in what he commands even if it were a sin, that would be an explicit violation of the laws. If the obligation is to obey him in other than sin against Allah—due to his saying صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: "There is no obedience to a creature in disobedience to the Creator"—then separating from the community would be permissible in some cases, which contradicts the beginning of the narration.

A similar problem applies to his saying at the end: "You must adhere to obedience in what Allah has commanded." If what is meant by "what Allah has commanded" is the obedience to the position of succession even if it involves sin, it would be an explicit violation of the legislation of laws. If what is meant is the obedience to Allah even if it necessitates disobedience to the position of succession, it would contradict the beginning of the narration.

It has become clear today through social research that the endorsement of a government by one who does not respect the prevailing sacred laws is something no rational and mature society would accept. Thus, it is mandatory to transcend the station of the Legislator of the religion from such a thing. To say that the interest of preserving the unity of the word and the agreement of the nation is more important than preserving some laws through separation means the permissibility of destroying the reality of the religion to preserve its name.

In al-Durr al-Manthur, also narrated by al-Tayalisi, Sa‘id ibn Mansur, Ahmad, ‘Abd ibn Humayd, al-Bukhari, Muslim, al-Tirmidhi, al-Nasa’i, Ibn Jarir, Ibn al-Mundhir, Ibn Abi Hatim, Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Bayhaqi in al-Asma’ wa al-Sifat, from Masruq, who said: I was reclining with ‘A’ishah when she said: "There are three things; whoever speaks of one of them has fabricated a great lie against Allah." I asked: "What are they?" She said: "Whoever claims that Muhammad saw his Lord has fabricated a great lie against Allah." He said: I was reclining, so I sat up and said: "O Mother of the Believers, bear with me and do not be hasty with me. Did Allah not say: وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ بِالْأُفُقِ الْمُبِينِ (He certainly saw him on the manifest horizon) and وَلَقَدْ رَآهُ نَزْلَةً أُخْرى (He certainly saw him yet another time)?"

She said: "I am the first of this nation to ask the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله about this. He said: 'It was Gabriel. I did not see him in the form in which he was created except these two times. I saw him descending from the sky, filling the greatness of his creation what is between the sky and the earth.'" She said: "Did you not hear Allah (عز وجل) say: لا تُدْرِكُهُ الْأَبْصارُ وَهُوَ يُدْرِكُ الْأَبْصارَ ـ وَهُوَ اللَّطِيفُ الْخَبِيرُ (The sights do not perceive Him, but He perceives the sights, and He is the All-attentive, the All-aware)? Or did you not hear Allah say: وَما كانَ لِبَشَرٍ أَنْ يُكَلِّمَهُ اللهُ إِلَّا وَحْياً (It is not [possible] for any human that Allah should speak to him except through revelation...) until His saying: عَلِيٌّ حَكِيمٌ (All-exalted, All-wise)?"

"And whoever claims that Muhammad concealed anything from the Book of Allah has fabricated a great lie against Allah, while Allah, majestic is His mention, says: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ ما أُنْزِلَ إِلَيْكَ مِنْ رَبِّكَ ـ وَإِنْ لَمْ تَفْعَلْ فَما بَلَّغْتَ رِسالَتَهُ وَاللهُ يَعْصِمُكَ مِنَ النَّاسِ (O Messenger! Proclaim what has been sent down to you from your Lord; and if you do not, you will not have proclaimed His message, and Allah shall protect you from people)."

She said: "And whoever claims that he informs people of what will be tomorrow has fabricated a great lie against Allah, while Allah, the Exalted, says: قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ (Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’)."

I say: There is something in the text of the narration. As for the verses of vision (الرؤية), they only negate sensory vision, not the vision of the heart (رؤية القلب), which is a type of vision beyond faith (الإيمان) that is mere conviction (الاعتقاد); we have discussed this extensively in the appropriate places.

As for His saying, the Exalted: يا أَيُّهَا الرَّسُولُ بَلِّغْ (O Messenger! Proclaim...), we have clarified in the commentary of that verse that it is specific, not general. Even if it were assumed to be general, it only indicates that everything sent down to him containing a message (رسالة) must be proclaimed. It is possible that something was sent down to him whose knowledge was exclusive to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, so he concealed it from others.

As for His saying: قُلْ لا يَعْلَمُ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَالْأَرْضِ الْغَيْبَ إِلَّا اللهُ (Say, ‘No one in the heavens and the earth knows the Unseen except Allah’), it only indicates the essential exclusivity (الاختصاص بالذات) of the knowledge of the Unseen to Him, the Exalted, like other verses of the exclusivity of the Unseen to Him. It does not negate another's knowledge of it through teaching from Him, the Exalted, as indicated by His saying: عالِمُ الْغَيْبِ فَلا يُظْهِرُ عَلى غَيْبِهِ أَحَداً إِلَّا مَنِ ارْتَضى مِنْ رَسُولٍ — Knower of the Unseen, He does not disclose His Unseen to anyone, except to an apostle He approves... (72:26-27). Allah, the Glorified, narrated a similar type of informing from the Messiah (peace be upon him) when he said: وَأُنَبِّئُكُمْ بِما تَأْكُلُونَ وَما تَدَّخِرُونَ — ...and I will inform you of what you eat and what you store up... (3:49). It is well known that the one who says the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله used to inform people of what would be tomorrow does not negate that this was through teaching from Allah to him.

The reports have reached the level of frequency (تواترت)—despite their dispersion and variety—from the paths of both sects regarding his صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله informing of many future events.

Ayahs

۞ وَإِذَا وَقَعَ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنَا لَهُمْ دَآبَّةً مِّنَ ٱلْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ ٱلنَّاسَ كَانُوا۟ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا لَا يُوقِنُونَ (٨٢)
When the word [of judgement] falls upon them, We shall bring out for them an Animal from the earth who shall tell them that the people had no faith in Our signs. (82)
وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِن كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجًا مِّمَّن يُكَذِّبُ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ (٨٣)
On that day We shall resurrect from every nation a group of those who denied Our signs, and they will be held in check. (83)
حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَا جَآءُو قَالَ أَكَذَّبْتُم بِـَٔايَـٰتِى وَلَمْ تُحِيطُوا۟ بِهَا عِلْمًا أَمَّاذَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٨٤)
When they come, He will say, ‘Did you deny My signs without comprehending them in knowledge? What was it that you used to do?’ (84)
وَوَقَعَ ٱلْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِم بِمَا ظَلَمُوا۟ فَهُمْ لَا يَنطِقُونَ (٨٥)
And the word [of judgement] shall fall upon them for their wrongdoing, and they will not speak. (85)
أَلَمْ يَرَوْا۟ أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا ٱلَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا۟ فِيهِ وَٱلنَّهَارَ مُبْصِرًا ۚ إِنَّ فِى ذَٰلِكَ لَـَٔايَـٰتٍ لِّقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ (٨٦)
Do they not see that We made the night that they may rest in it, and the day to provide visibility. There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith. (86)
وَيَوْمَ يُنفَخُ فِى ٱلصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَن فِى ٱلسَّمَـٰوَٰتِ وَمَن فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَن شَآءَ ٱللَّهُ ۚ وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ دَٰخِرِينَ (٨٧)
The day when the trumpet is blown, whoever is in the heavens and whoever is on the earth will be terrified, except such as Allah wishes, and all will come to Him in utter humility. (87)
وَتَرَى ٱلْجِبَالَ تَحْسَبُهَا جَامِدَةً وَهِىَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ ٱلسَّحَابِ ۚ صُنْعَ ٱللَّهِ ٱلَّذِىٓ أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَىْءٍ ۚ إِنَّهُۥ خَبِيرٌۢ بِمَا تَفْعَلُونَ (٨٨)
You see the mountains, which you suppose, to be stationary, while they drift like passing clouds—the handiwork of Allah who has made everything faultless. He is indeed well aware of what you do. (88)
مَن جَآءَ بِٱلْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُۥ خَيْرٌ مِّنْهَا وَهُم مِّن فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ ءَامِنُونَ (٨٩)
Whoever brings virtue shall receive [a reward] better than it; and on that day they will be secure from terror. (89)
وَمَن جَآءَ بِٱلسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِى ٱلنَّارِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا مَا كُنتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٠)
But whoever brings vice—they shall be cast on their faces into the Fire [and told:] ‘Shall you not be requited for what you used to do?’ (90)
إِنَّمَآ أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ رَبَّ هَـٰذِهِ ٱلْبَلْدَةِ ٱلَّذِى حَرَّمَهَا وَلَهُۥ كُلُّ شَىْءٍ ۖ وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٩١)
[Say], ‘I have been commanded to worship the Lord of this city who has made it inviolable and to whom all things belong, and I have been commanded to be among those who submit [to Allah], (91)
وَأَنْ أَتْلُوَا۟ ٱلْقُرْءَانَ ۖ فَمَنِ ٱهْتَدَىٰ فَإِنَّمَا يَهْتَدِى لِنَفْسِهِۦ ۖ وَمَن ضَلَّ فَقُلْ إِنَّمَآ أَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُنذِرِينَ (٩٢)
and to recite the Quran.’ Whoever is guided is guided only for his own good, and as for him who goes astray, say, ‘I am just one of the warners.’ (92)
وَقُلِ ٱلْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ سَيُرِيكُمْ ءَايَـٰتِهِۦ فَتَعْرِفُونَهَا ۚ وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغَـٰفِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ (٩٣)
And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah. Soon He will show you His signs, and you will recognize them.’ Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do. (93)

(Commentary)

These verses are a continuation of the previous section of verses. They point toward the Resurrection (البعث) — the raising of the dead for judgment — and some of the events associated with it, as well as some of its portents (أشراط). The Surah concludes by returning to its opening themes of warning (الإنذار) and giving glad tidings (التبشير).

His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ — "When the word falls upon them, We shall bring out for them a creature from the earth who will speak to them that the people had no faith in Our signs." (27:82) The context (السياق) — given that the verse is connected to the preceding verses discussing the polytheists (المشركين) who were contemporaries of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, or specifically the people of Mecca from the Quraysh, who were the most severe in their enmity toward the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and his call — necessitates that the pronouns in "upon them" (عَلَيْهِمْ), "for them" (لَهُمْ), and "speak to them" (تُكَلِّمُهُمْ) refer to the polytheists being discussed. However, it does not refer to them exclusively, but rather insofar as they are people addressed by the call. Thus, the reality intended is the generality of the people of this nation (الأمة) in terms of their unity; what applies to the first of them in judgment applies to the last of them. This type of rhetorical regard (العناية) occurs frequently in the speech of the Exalted.

The meaning of "the word falling upon them" (وقوع القول عليهم) is the realization of the referent (المصداق) of the word regarding them, and their becoming specified for its truth to apply to them, as in the following verse: وَوَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِما ظَلَمُوا — "And the word shall fall upon them because of the wrong they have done" (27:85), meaning the punishment became due upon them. Thus, the sentence carries the meaning of "the word became due against them" (حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ), a phrase that appears frequently in His speech. The difference between the two expressions is that the emphasis in "the word fell upon them" (وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ) is on their specification as the referent of the word, while in "the word became due against them" (حَقَّ عَلَيْهِمُ الْقَوْلُ), it is on the settled establishment and fixity of the word regarding them such that it does not depart.

As for what this "word" falling upon them is, that which is suitable from His speech to interpret it is His saying: سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَفِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ — "Soon We shall show them Our signs in the horizons and in their own souls until it becomes clear to them that He is the Real" (41:53). For the "signs" (الآيات) He will show them are certainly other than the celestial and terrestrial signs that are constantly within their sight and hearing; rather, they are certain miraculous signs (آيات خارقة للعادة) to which their souls will submit and be compelled to believe in, at a time when they do not have certainty (لا يوقنون) in any of the signs of the heavens and the earth that are before their eyes and under their observation.

From this, it becomes clear that His saying: أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ — "that the people had no faith in Our signs" is a causal explanation (تعليل) for the word falling upon them, the implied meaning being "because the people..." His use of "were" (كانُوا) serves to indicate the settled state of their lack of certainty. The "signs" intended here are the witnessed signs of the heavens and the earth, other than the miraculous signs. It has also been recited as أَنَّ with a kasrah on the hamzah (إِنَّ), which is more preponderant than the recitation with fathah; this supports what we have mentioned, as the sentence itself would then be a causal explanation without the need to imply the letter lam (ل).

His saying: أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهُمْ — "We shall bring out for them a creature from the earth who will speak to them" is an exposition of a miraculous sign among the signs promised in His saying: سَنُرِيهِمْ آياتِنا فِي الْآفاقِ وَفِي أَنْفُسِهِمْ حَتَّى يَتَبَيَّنَ لَهُمْ أَنَّهُ الْحَقُ. The fact that it is described as a miraculous matter indicates that "bringing out from the earth" (الإخراج من الأرض) refers either to restoration to life and resurrection after death, or something close to it. As for it being a "creature" (دابة) that speaks to them, a creature (الدابة) is anything that crawls upon the earth among living beings, whether human or otherwise. If it were a human, his speaking to the people would be according to the norm; if it were a non-human animal, its speaking would be, like its emergence from the earth, a miracle (خرقاً للعادة).

We do not find in His speech anything suitable to interpret this verse or to identify what this creature is that He will bring out from the earth to speak to them. What is its description? How does it emerge? What does it say? Rather, the context of the verse is the best evidence that the intent is ambiguity (الإبهام); it is a symbolic discourse (كلام مرموز).

The summary of the meaning is: when the affair of the people reaches the point—and it shall reach it—where they no longer have certainty in Our signs witnessed by them, and their capacity for faith through intellect and reflection is nullified, the time will have come for Us to show them what We promised to show of the miraculous signs that clarify the truth to them such that they are compelled to acknowledge it; thus, We shall bring out for them a creature from the earth who will speak to them.

This is what the context provides and what reflection upon the verse guides to regarding its meaning. The exegetes have gone to extremes in their disagreement over the meanings of the individual words and sentences of the verse, the overall conclusion, the reality of this creature, its description, the meaning of its speaking, the manner of its emergence, the time of its emergence, the number of its emergences, and the place from which it emerges, resulting in many opinions that have no basis except arbitrary judgment (التحكم). Therefore, we have refrained from quoting or discussing them; whoever wishes to examine them should consult the lengthy commentaries.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجاً مِمَّنْ يُكَذِّبُ بِآياتِنا فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ — "On the day We shall muster from every nation a group of those who deny Our signs, and they shall be held in check" (27:83). A group (الفوج), as mentioned by al-Raghib, is a passing and hurrying company. Holding in check (الإيزاع) is to halt the people and detain them such that the first of them is held back for the last of them to catch up.

His saying: وَيَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ — "On the day We shall muster" is in the accusative case as an adverb of time (ظرفية) for an omitted verb, the implied meaning being "And remember the day We shall muster." The "mustering" (الحشر) intended here is the gathering after death, because those mustered are a group (فوج) from every nation, and there is no gathering of all nations at one time while they are alive. The word "from" (مِنْ) in His saying "from every nation" (مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ) denotes partitivity (التبعيض), and in His saying "of those who deny" (مِمَّنْ يُكَذِّبُ), it denotes either clarification (التبيين) or partitivity.

The "signs" in His saying "deny Our signs" (يُكَذِّبُ بِآياتِنا) refer to all signs indicating the Origin (المبدأ) and the Return (المعاد) — the beginning of creation and the resurrection — including the prophets, the Imams, and the heavenly books, rather than the Hour (الساعة) and what occurs within it or at its establishment, and rather than the Quranic verses alone. This is because the mustering is not restricted to the Islamic nation, but rather involves groups from various nations.

It is strange that some insist that the discourse is explicit (نص) in that the "signs" here and in the following verse refer to the Quranic verses. They argue: "Because they are what contain the proofs of truthfulness which they did not encompass, despite the obligation to contemplate and reflect upon them, unlike the Hour and what it contains." End quote.

The falsity of this is obvious, for the fact that things like the Hour are not intended does not necessitate that the Quranic verses are intended, especially since it is clear that those mustered are groups from all nations, and the Quran is but a book for one group among them.

The apparent meaning of the verse is that this mustering (الحشر) occurs on a day other than the Day of Resurrection, because it is a mustering of some from every nation, not all of them, whereas Allah the Exalted said in describing the mustering on the Day of Resurrection: وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً — "and We shall muster them and not leave out any one of them" (18:47).

It has been said: The mustering intended here is the mustering for punishment after the general mustering that encompasses all creation; thus, it is a mustering after a mustering.

The objection to this is that if the mustering to punishment were intended, it would be necessary to mention this end-goal to remove ambiguity, as in His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُحْشَرُ أَعْداءُ اللهِ إِلَى النَّارِ فَهُمْ يُوزَعُونَ حَتَّى إِذا ما جاؤُها — "On the day when the enemies of Allah are mustered toward the Fire, they shall be held in check, until when they come to it..." (41:19-20). Yet, nothing is mentioned after this verse except rebuke and the decisive judgment, not punishment. The verse, as you see, is absolute (مطلقة); it contains no indication of this specific mustering mentioned. Its absolute nature is further increased by His saying afterward: حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُ — "until when they come", for He did not say: "until when they come to the punishment" or "the Fire" or anything else.

This is also supported by the fact that this verse and the two following it occur after the news of the Creature of the Earth (دابة الأرض), which is one of the portents of the Hour, and before His saying: وَيَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ — "On the day the Trumpet is blown" until the end of the verses describing the events of the Day of Resurrection. There is no sense in mentioning an event from the events of the Day of Resurrection before mentioning its commencement and the occurrence of the general events within it. The chronological order of occurrence (الترتيب الوقوعي) would require mentioning the mustering of a group from every nation—if it were an event of the Day of Resurrection—after mentioning the blowing of the Trumpet and their coming to Him in abasement.

Some who interpreted the verse as the mustering on the Day of Resurrection noticed this problem and said: "Perhaps the mention of this event was placed before the blowing of the Trumpet and the occurrence of the Great Event to signal that each of the states contained in this and that is a supreme calamity (طامة كبرى) and a dire disaster worthy of being mentioned independently. Had the chronological order been observed, it might have been imagined that the whole is a single disaster."

As you know, this is a contrived and unconvincing justification. If it were as mentioned, then preventing the illusion that the mustering mentioned in the verse occurs on a day other than the Day of Resurrection—by placing the verse after the verse of the blowing of the Trumpet while mentioning that which removes the said ambiguity—would have been more worthy of consideration than preventing the illusion they imagined.

Thus, it has become clear that the verse apparently indicates that this mustering mentioned within it occurs before the Day of Resurrection, even if it is not an explicit text (نص) that admits no interpretation.

His saying, the Exalted: حَتَّى إِذا جاؤُ قالَ أَكَذَّبْتُمْ بِآياتِي وَلَمْ تُحِيطُوا بِها عِلْماً أَمَّا ذا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "When they come, He will say, ‘Did you deny My signs without having encompassed them in knowledge? Or what was it that you used to do?’" (27:84). The "coming" (المجيء) — with the aid of the context — refers to being present at the station of address indicated by His saying: "He will say, 'Did you deny...'" etc. The "signs" (الآيات) — as previously mentioned in the preceding verse — refer to all signs indicating the truth. His saying: وَلَمْ تُحِيطُوا بِها عِلْماً — "without having encompassed them in knowledge" is a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية), meaning: you denied them while you had no knowledge of them because of your turning away from them. How could you deny what you do not know? That is, you branded them with falsehood and lack of indication without knowledge. His saying: أَمَّا ذا كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "Or what was it that you used to do?" means: other than denial.

The meaning is: until when they are present at the station of address, Allah, the Glorified, will say to them: Did you deny My signs while you had not encompassed them in knowledge, or what thing were you doing other than denial? In this is a rebuke to them that they were occupied with nothing other than denying the signs of Allah, without any excusable distraction keeping them from it.

His saying, the Exalted: وَوَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ بِما ظَلَمُوا فَهُمْ لا يَنْطِقُونَ — "And the word shall fall upon them because of the wrong they have done, and they shall not speak." (27:85). The letter ba in "because of what they wronged" (بِما ظَلَمُوا) denotes causality (السببية), and the word ma is an infinitive particle (مصدرية), meaning: the word fell upon them because of their being wrongdoers. His saying: فَهُمْ لا يَنْطِقُونَ — "and they shall not speak" is a result (تفريع) of the word falling upon them.

This supports the view that the "word" (القول) that falls upon them is His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ — "Indeed Allah does not guide the wrongdoing lot" (6:144). The meaning is: because they were wrongdoers in their denial of the signs, they were not guided to that which they could use as an excuse; thus, they were cut off from speech and they do not speak.

The falling of the word upon them has also been interpreted as the necessity of punishment upon them. More appropriate according to this is that the "word" falling upon them is His decree (قضاؤه) of punishment for the wrongdoers in verses such as: أَلا إِنَّ الظَّالِمِينَ فِي عَذابٍ مُقِيمٍ — "Behold, the wrongdoers are in a lasting punishment" (42:45). The meaning would be: because they were wrongdoers, punishment was decreed for them, so they had nothing to say. The previous view is more fitting.

As for interpreting the falling of the word as the descent of punishment and entry into the Fire, it is far from the context because it does not harmonize with the resultative clause in His saying: فَهُمْ لا يَنْطِقُونَ — "and they shall not speak."

His saying, the Exalted: أَلَمْ يَرَوْا أَنَّا جَعَلْنَا اللَّيْلَ لِيَسْكُنُوا فِيهِ وَالنَّهارَ مُبْصِراً إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "Do they not see that We have made the night for them to rest in it, and the day to afford sight? There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith." (27:86). Since He described in the previous verses that many people are in deafness and blindness from hearing the word of truth and looking at the signs of Allah and reflecting upon them, then mentioned the Creature of the Earth and that He will bring it out as a miraculous sign that speaks to them, then mentioned that He will muster a group from every nation of the deniers and rebuke them so that the proof is established against them for their speaking without knowledge of the signs due to their turning away from them—He rebukes them in this verse and blames them for their denial of the signs despite their ignorance, for they used to see the night in which they naturally rest, and that there is a day affording sight through which the signs of the heavens and the earth appear to them; why then did they not gain insight?

His saying: إِنَّ فِي ذلِكَ لَآياتٍ لِقَوْمٍ يُؤْمِنُونَ — "There are indeed signs in that for a people who have faith" means: in the making of the night as a rest in which they reside and the day as affording sight in which they see the signs of the heavens and the earth, there are signs for a people who possess the quality of submission and belief in the truth that appears to them.

The "signs" (الآيات) refer to the marks and aspects within them that indicate monotheism (التوحيد) — the absolute oneness of Allah in His essence, attributes, and acts — and the realities of knowledge that follow it. Among these is their indication that man ought to remain still in that which is meant for stillness, which is the night that casts the veil of its darkness over the eyes, and to move in that which is meant for movement, which is the day affording sight through which things containing the benefits of life appear to the eyes.

Thus, man must remain silent regarding that which the darkness of ignorance has veiled from him, and not speak without knowledge, nor deny that which he has not encompassed in knowledge; and he should speak and believe in that which the clear signs manifest for him, which are like the day affording sight.

His saying, the Exalted: وَيَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ فِي الصُّورِ فَفَزِعَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ — "On the day the Trumpet is blown, everyone who is in the heavens and everyone who is on the earth will be struck with terror, except those whom Allah wishes, and all shall come to Him in utter abasement." (27:87). The blowing of the Trumpet (النفخ في الصور) is a metaphor for informing a large group, like an army, of what they must do collectively, such as gathering, departing, and so on. Terror (الفزع), as al-Raghib said, is a contraction and aversion that overcomes a person from something frightening; it is of the same genus as distress (الجزع). Abasement (الدخور) is humiliation and smallness.

It has been said: The blowing intended here is the second blowing of the Trumpet by which life is breathed into the bodies and they are resurrected for the decisive judgment. This is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ — "and all shall come to Him in utter abasement", referring to their presence before Allah, the Glorified. It is also supported by His exception of مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ — "those whom Allah wishes" from the judgment of terror, and then His saying regarding the one who brings a good deed: وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ — "and they will be secure from the terror of that day", as it indicates that the terror mentioned is the terror in the second blowing.

It has also been said: The blowing intended is the first blowing by which the living die, based on His saying: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ فَصَعِقَ مَنْ فِي السَّماواتِ وَمَنْ فِي الْأَرْضِ إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ ثُمَّ نُفِخَ فِيهِ أُخْرى فَإِذا هُمْ قِيامٌ يَنْظُرُونَ — "And the Trumpet will be blown, and everyone who is in the heavens and everyone who is on the earth will swoon, except those whom Allah wishes. Then it will be blown another time, and behold, they will be standing, looking on." (39:68). For the swooning (الصعقة) is from terror and has been arranged upon the first blowing. According to this, the meaning of His saying: وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ — "and all shall come to Him in utter abasement" would be their return to Allah, the Glorified, through death.

It is not unlikely that the blowing of the Trumpet intended on that day is the blowing in an absolute sense, encompassing both that which causes death and that which gives life, for the blowing, in any case, is among the specifics of the Hour. Thus, what is mentioned of the terror of some and the security of others from terror, and the moving of the mountains, would be among the properties of the first blowing; and what is mentioned of their coming in abasement would be among the properties of the second blowing. This resolves the objections raised against each of the two previous views.

Allah, the Glorified, has excepted a group of His servants from the judgment of general terror that encompasses those in the heavens and the earth. A discussion on the meaning of this exception will follow in the commentary on the upcoming verse: وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ — "and they will be secure from the terror of that day."

The apparent meaning is that His saying: وَكُلٌّ أَتَوْهُ داخِرِينَ — "and all shall come to Him in utter abasement" refers to the return of all who are in the heavens and the earth, even those excepted from the judgment of terror, and their presence before Him, the Exalted. As for His saying: فَإِنَّهُمْ لَمُحْضَرُونَ إِلَّا عِبادَ اللهِ الْمُخْلَصِينَ — "they will indeed be arraigned [for punishment], except the chosen servants of Allah" (37:127-128), the apparent meaning is the denial of their being arraigned in the gathering for accounting and questioning, not the denial of their resurrection, their return to Allah, and their presence before Him. For the verses of the Resurrection are explicit regarding the universality of the resurrection for all creatures such that not a single one is left out.

The attribution of abasement (الدخور) and humiliation to His friends (أوليائه) does not contradict the might (العزة) they have with Allah; for the might of the servant with Allah is his humiliation before Him, and his wealth through Allah is his poverty toward Him. Indeed, the humiliation of His enemies—due to the false might they see for themselves—is a humiliation of disgrace (ذلة هوان).

His saying, the Exalted: وَتَرَى الْجِبالَ تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ صُنْعَ اللهِ الَّذِي أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَفْعَلُونَ — "You see the mountains, which you suppose to be stationary, while they drift like clouds—the handiwork of Allah who has perfected everything. Indeed He is aware of what you do." (27:88). Since the verse is situated within the context of the verses of the Resurrection and surrounded by them, it describes some of the signs that occur on that day, which is the moving of the mountains. Allah the Exalted has also said regarding this meaning: وَسُيِّرَتِ الْجِبالُ فَكانَتْ سَراباً — "and the mountains shall be set in motion and become a mirage" (78:20), among others.

Thus, His saying: وَتَرَى الْجِبالَ — "You see the mountains" is an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and the intent is to depict the event, as in His saying: وَتَرَى النَّاسَ سُكارى — "and you see the people drunk" (22:2), meaning: this is their witnessed state on this day; you would see it if you were an observer. His saying: تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً — "which you suppose to be stationary" means: you think them now—before the Resurrection has yet been established—to be stationary and not moving. The sentence is either parenthetical (معترضة) or circumstantial (حالية).

His saying: وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ — "while they drift like clouds" is a circumstantial clause for the mountains, and its governing verb is "you see" (تَرَى); that is, you see them when the Trumpet is blown while they are moving like the movement of clouds in the sky.

His saying: صُنْعَ اللهِ الَّذِي أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "the handiwork of Allah who has perfected everything" is an absolute object (مفعول مطلق) for an omitted verb, meaning: He fashioned it with a fashioning. In the sentence is an allusion that this handiwork and act of His, the Exalted, is a destruction of the world and a demolition of the universe, yet it is in reality its completion and the perfection of its system. This is because of what results from it: the bringing of everything to its end-goal and its arrival at the destination it is facing, whether of felicity or wretchedness. For that is the handiwork of Allah who has perfected everything; He, the Glorified, does not strip perfection from what He has perfected, nor does He allow corruption to overcome what He has set right. Thus, in the destruction of the world is the building of the Hereafter.

His saying: إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَفْعَلُونَ — "Indeed He is aware of what you do." It has been said: This is a causal explanation for the fact that the mentioned blowing of the Trumpet and what follows it is a firm handiwork of His, the Exalted. For His knowledge of the outward and inward actions of the legally responsible (المكلفين) necessitates their manifestation and the clarification of their qualities—whether good or evil—and the arrangement of their effects of reward and punishment upon them after the resurrection, the mustering, and the moving of the mountains.

You see the artificiality (التكلف) in this and that the context after all that does not accept it.

It has also been said: His saying: إِنَّهُ خَبِيرٌ بِما تَفْعَلُونَ — "Indeed He is aware of what you do" is a new sentence (استئناف) serving as an answer to an implied question, as if it were asked: "What will happen after these calamities?" It was said: Indeed Allah is aware of the deeds of the doers, so He will recompense them for their deeds. This was then detailed by His saying: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it" until the end of the two verses.

There is another view here derived from careful reflection on the context of the previous verses. In them, Allah, the Glorified, commanded His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to rely upon Him and to return the affair of the polytheists and the Children of Israel to Him. For he can only guide the believers in His signs who submit to the truth. As for the polytheists in their denial and the Children of Israel in their disagreement, they are dead and do not hear; they are deaf and blind, they do not hear nor are they guided to the truth by looking at the signs of the heavens and the earth and reflecting upon them by their own choice.

Then He mentioned what they will face—given that their state is such that the signs do not affect them—and that He will bring out for them a creature from the earth who will speak to them, which is a miraculous sign that compels them to accept the truth. He also mentioned that He will muster from every nation a group of the deniers and establish the proof against them. Ultimately, He is aware of their deeds and will recompense whoever brings a good or evil deed with his work on the day the Trumpet is blown, when they are struck with terror and come to Him in abasement.

By reflecting on this context, it appears that it is more appropriate for "the day it is blown" (يَوْمَ يُنْفَخُ) to be an adverbial qualifier for His saying: "Indeed He is aware of what they do" (إنه خبير بما يفعلون). The recitation of "they do" (يفعلون) with the third-person ya is more preponderant than the common recitation in the second person.

The meaning is: And He, the Exalted, is aware of what the people of the heavens and the earth do on the day the Trumpet is blown and they come to Him in abasement. He recompenses whoever brings a good deed with better than it, and whoever brings an evil deed by casting their faces into the Fire, each recompensed by his deed. According to this, the verse carries the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: أَفَلا يَعْلَمُ إِذا بُعْثِرَ ما فِي الْقُبُورِ وَحُصِّلَ ما فِي الصُّدُورِ إِنَّ رَبَّهُمْ بِهِمْ يَوْمَئِذٍ لخَبِيرٌ — "Does he not know, when what is in the graves is overturned, and what is in the breasts is gathered, that their Lord will indeed be aware of them on that day?" (100:9-11), and His saying: يَوْمَ هُمْ بارِزُونَ لا يَخْفى عَلَى اللهِ مِنْهُمْ شَيْءٌ — "The day when they will emerge [from their graves], nothing about them will be hidden from Allah" (40:16). His saying: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ — "Whoever brings a good deed" etc., would be a detailing of His saying: "Indeed He is aware of what they do" in terms of the necessary consequence of awareness, which is recompense for what was done and worked, as He indicated at the end by His saying: هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "Will you be recompensed for anything except what you used to do?". The shift (الالتفات) from the third person to the second person in His saying: "Will you be recompensed..." etc., is to intensify the rebuke and reprimand.

Regarding the verse, namely His saying: وَتَرَى الْجِبالَ تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً وَهِيَ تَمُرُّ مَرَّ السَّحابِ — "You see the mountains, which you suppose to be stationary, while they drift like clouds" etc., there are two other opinions:

First: Interpreting it as substantial motion (الحركة الجوهرية) — the continuous transformation and movement within the very essence of things — and that things like mountains move by their essence toward the goal of their existence, which is their mustering and return to Allah, the Glorified.

This meaning is more appropriate considering the allusion in His saying: "you suppose them to be stationary" (تَحْسَبُها جامِدَةً) that they are moving today, before the Resurrection has been established. As for making the Day of Resurrection the time for both the supposition of stationarity and the drifting like clouds together, it is something not to be considered.

Second: Interpreting it as the translational motion (حركة انتقالية) of the earth. This is a good meaning considering the verse in itself, but firstly: it causes a disconnection of the verse from what precedes and follows it of the verses of the Resurrection; and secondly: it breaks the connection of His saying: "Indeed He is aware of what you do" with what precedes it.

His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it, and they will be secure from the terror of that day." (27:89). This verse and the one after it — as previously indicated — are a detailing of His saying: "Indeed He is aware of what you do" in terms of its effect, which is recompense. The meaning of His saying: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it" is that he has a reward that is better than the good deed he brought. This is because the deed, whatever it may be, is a means to the reward intended for its sake, and the purpose and goal are in any case superior to the means.

His saying: وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ — "and they will be secure from the terror of that day." The apparent context is that this terror is the terror after the second blowing of the Trumpet, not the first. Thus, it carries the meaning of His saying: لا يَحْزُنُهُمُ الْفَزَعُ الْأَكْبَرُ وَتَتَلَقَّاهُمُ الْمَلائِكَةُ هذا يَوْمُكُمُ الَّذِي كُنْتُمْ تُوعَدُونَ — "The Great Terror will not grieve them, and the angels will receive them [saying]: ‘This is your day which you were promised.’" (21:103).

His saying, the Exalted: وَمَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "And whoever brings an evil deed, they will be cast down on their faces into the Fire: ‘Are you recompensed for anything except what you used to do?’" (27:90). It is said: "He cast him on his face" (كبه على وجهه) and "he was cast down" (فانكب), meaning he threw him on his face and he fell upon it. Thus, the attribution of casting to their faces is a rational metaphor (مجاز عقلي), and the origin is: they were cast upon their faces.

His saying: هَلْ تُجْزَوْنَ إِلَّا ما كُنْتُمْ تَعْمَلُونَ — "Are you recompensed for anything except what you used to do?" The interrogation is for denial (الإنكار). The meaning is: this recompense of yours is nothing but the very deed you performed, manifested to you and made binding upon you; thus, there is no injustice in the recompense nor any iniquity in the judgment.

The two verses are in the position of explaining what is in the nature of the good deed (الحسنة) and the evil deed (السيئة) regarding recompense. They contain the judgment of one who brings only a good deed and one who is encompassed by sin and overwhelmed by evil. As for one who carries both a good deed and an evil deed, his judgment is known from this in general, while the detail is found elsewhere.

His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ رَبَّ هذِهِ الْبَلْدَةِ الَّذِي حَرَّمَها وَلَهُ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ — "I am only commanded to worship the Lord of this city, who has made it inviolable, and to Him belongs everything." (27:91). These three verses — from here to the end of the Surah — are the conclusion of the Surah. In them, it is explained that this true call is a glad tiding and a warning in which the proof is completed, without any of their affair returning to him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله. Rather, the affair belongs to Allah, and He will show them His signs so they will recognize them; He is not heedless of their deeds.

In His saying: إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ — "I am only commanded" etc., the speech is from the tongue of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله; thus, it carries the meaning: "Say: I am only commanded to worship the Lord of this city." The city referred to by this indication is the Noble Mecca. In the discourse is an honoring of it from two aspects: the attribution of the Lord to it, and its description as inviolable (حرمة) where He said: "the Lord of this city who has made it inviolable." In it is also a subtle reproach (تعريض) to them, as they disbelieved in this blessing—the blessing of the inviolability of their city—and did not thank Allah by worshipping Him, but instead turned to the worship of idols.

His saying: وَلَهُ كُلُّ شَيْءٍ — "and to Him belongs everything" is an indication of the vastness of His kingdom, the Exalted, to repel what might be imagined: that He only owns Mecca of which He is the Lord. In that case, His state would be like that of the other idols, each of which, according to their belief, owns a part of the parts of the world, such as the heaven, the earth, such-and-such a city, such-and-such a people, or such-and-such a family. Thus, the Exalted would be a deity worshipped like one of the gods, situated in their rank and alongside them.

His saying: وَأُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَكُونَ مِنَ الْمُسْلِمِينَ — "and I am commanded to be among those who submit" means: among those who have submitted to Him in what He willed; and He wills nothing except that which creation guides to and the primordial nature (الفطرة) — the innate disposition toward truth that Allah placed in every human being — calls out for, which is the upright, primordial religion (الدين الحنيف الفطري) that is the creed of Abraham.

His saying, the Exalted: وَأَنْ أَتْلُوَا الْقُرْآنَ فَمَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَقُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ — "and to recite the Quran.’ So whoever is guided is guided only for [the good of] his own soul, and as for him who goes astray, say, ‘I am only one of the warners.’" (27:92). This is coordinated with His saying: "to worship" (أَنْ أَعْبُدَ), meaning: I am commanded to read the Quran. The intent is its recitation to them, as evidenced by the resultative clause in His saying: "So whoever is guided..." etc.

His saying: فَمَنِ اهْتَدى فَإِنَّما يَهْتَدِي لِنَفْسِهِ — "So whoever is guided is guided only for [the good of] his own soul" means: whoever is guided by this Quran, the one who benefits from it is his own soul, and its benefit does not return to me.

His saying: وَمَنْ ضَلَّ فَقُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ — "and as for him who goes astray, say, ‘I am only one of the warners.’" means: whoever is not guided by it by turning away from the remembrance of his Lord—which is misguidance—then upon him is his misguidance and the evil consequence of his disbelief, not upon me. For I am nothing but a warner commanded with that, and I am not a guardian over him; Allah is the Guardian over him.

The shift from what would be expected, such as "and whoever goes astray, then I am only among the warners," to His saying: فَقُلْ إِنَّما أَنَا مِنَ الْمُنْذِرِينَ — "say, ‘I am only one of the warners’" is to remind him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله of the covenant previously made with him: that he is nothing but a warner and nothing of their affair belongs to him. Thus, he must rely upon his Lord and return their affair to Him, as He said: فَتَوَكَّلْ عَلَى اللهِ إِنَّكَ عَلَى الْحَقِّ الْمُبِينِ إِنَّكَ لا تُسْمِعُ الْمَوْتى — "So put your trust in Allah; indeed you stand on the manifest truth. You cannot make the dead hear..." (27:79-80). It is as if it were said: And whoever goes astray, say to him: You have heard that my Lord has not placed upon me anything except warning; thus, I am not responsible for the misguidance of whoever goes astray.

His saying, the Exalted: وَقُلِ الْحَمْدُ لِلَّهِ سَيُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ فَتَعْرِفُونَها وَما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — "And say, ‘All praise belongs to Allah. He will soon show you His signs, and you will recognize them.’ Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do." (27:93). This is coordinated with His saying: "say, ‘I am only one of the warners.’" In it is a return to what He mentioned after commanding His Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله to rely upon Him regarding their affair: that He will make an evil end for the polytheists, judge between the Children of Israel in what they disagreed upon, show them His signs which they will be compelled to believe, and then recompense them for their deeds.

The summary of the meaning is: And say: Beautiful praise (الثناء الجميل) belongs to Allah the Exalted in what He carries out in His kingdom, as He called the people to that which contains their good and felicity, and guided those who believed in His signs and submitted to Him. As for the deniers, He caused their hearts to die, made their ears deaf, and blinded their eyes; so they went astray and denied His signs.

His saying: سَيُرِيكُمْ آياتِهِ فَتَعْرِفُونَها — "He will soon show you His signs, and you will recognize them" is an indication of what preceded in His saying: وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ — "When the word falls upon them, We shall bring out for them a creature from the earth" and what follows it. The apparent generality of His saying "His signs" (آياتِهِ) is evidence that it encompasses all the signs that compel them to accept the truth, among those that appear to them before the establishment of the Hour and after it.

His saying: وَما رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — "Your Lord is not oblivious of what you do" is an address to the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, and it is in the position of a causal explanation for what preceded it. That is: your deeds, O assembly of servants, are before the eye of your Lord; nothing escapes Him of what wisdom necessitates in response to your deeds—of calling, guidance, misguidance, and showing signs—then the recompense of the virtuous among you and the evildoers on the Day of Resurrection.

It has also been recited as "what they do" (عما يعملون) with the third-person ya, and perhaps it is more preponderant; its meaning is a threat to the deniers. In His saying: "your Lord" (رَبُّكَ), by adding "Lord" to the second-person pronoun, there is a comforting of the soul of the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله and a strengthening of his side.

(Narrative Discussion)

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ — "When the word falls upon them" (the verse): My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Abu Basir, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "The Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله came upon Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him) while he was sleeping in the mosque; he had gathered some sand and placed his head upon it. He moved him with his foot and then said: 'Rise, O Creature of the Earth (دابة الأرض)!' A man from his companions said: 'O Messenger of Allah, may some of us call others by this name?' He replied: 'No, by Allah, it is for him specifically. He is the Creature (الدابة) whom Allah mentioned in His Book, saying: وَإِذا وَقَعَ الْقَوْلُ عَلَيْهِمْ أَخْرَجْنا لَهُمْ دَابَّةً مِنَ الْأَرْضِ تُكَلِّمُهمْ أَنَّ النَّاسَ كانُوا بِآياتِنا لا يُوقِنُونَ.'"

Then he said: "O ‘Ali, when it is the end of time, Allah will bring you out in the most beautiful form, and with you will be a branding iron (ميسم) with which you will brand your enemies."

A man said to Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him): "The commonality (العامة) — the non-Shi'a Muslims — say that this verse means 'He will wound them' (تَكْلِمُهُمْ)." Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him) replied: "May Allah 'wound' them in the fire of Hell! It is only 'speaking to them' (تُكَلِّمُهُمْ) from speech (الكلام)."

I say: The narrations carrying this meaning are numerous through Shi'i chains.

In al-Majma‘, it is narrated that Muhammad ibn Ka‘b al-Qurazi said: "‘Ali was asked about the Creature, and he said: 'By Allah, it has no tail, but it certainly has a beard.'"

I say: There are many narrations describing its physical form that include wonders, yet they are contradictory. Whoever wishes to see them should refer to the compendiums of hadith like al-Durr al-Manthur or the lengthy commentaries like Ruh al-Ma‘ani.

In Tafsir al-Qummi: My father narrated to me from Ibn Abi ‘Umayr, from Hammad, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "What do the people say regarding this verse: يَوْمَ نَحْشُرُ مِنْ كُلِّ أُمَّةٍ فَوْجاً — 'On the day We shall muster from every nation a group'?" I said: "They say it is on the Day of Resurrection." He said: "It is not as they say; it is regarding the Return (الرجعة) — the restoration to life of a group of the virtuous and the wicked before the Day of Resurrection. Would Allah muster from every nation a group on the Day of Resurrection and leave the rest? The verse of the Resurrection is: وَحَشَرْناهُمْ فَلَمْ نُغادِرْ مِنْهُمْ أَحَداً — 'and We shall muster them and not leave out any one of them'."

I say: The reports concerning the Return (الرجعة) through Shi'i chains are very numerous.

In al-Majma‘, regarding His saying, the Exalted: وَنُفِخَ فِي الصُّورِ — "And the Trumpet will be blown": There is disagreement regarding the meaning of the Trumpet (الصور)... until he said: "It is said: it is a horn that is blown into, resembling a bugle—and that has been mentioned in the hadith."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِلَّا مَنْ شاءَ اللهُ — "except those whom Allah wishes": It is said: "It means the martyrs (الشهداء), for they will not be struck with terror on that day." This has been narrated in a marfu‘ report — a narration attributed directly to the Prophet.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: صُنْعَ اللهِ الَّذِي أَتْقَنَ كُلَّ شَيْءٍ — "the handiwork of Allah who has perfected everything": He said: "The act of Allah who has made everything firm."

In the same source, regarding His saying, the Exalted: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ وَمَنْ جاءَ بِالسَّيِّئَةِ فَكُبَّتْ وُجُوهُهُمْ فِي النَّارِ — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it, and they will be secure from the terror of that day. And whoever brings an evil deed, they will be cast down on their faces into the Fire": He said: "The good deed (الحسنة), by Allah, is the guardianship (ولاية) of Amir al-Mu'minin (peace be upon him), and the evil deed (السيئة), by Allah, is enmity toward him."

I say: This is of the nature of application (الجري) — applying the verse to its most prominent instance — and is not the literal interpretation (تفسير). There are many narrations with this content, which can perhaps be interpreted according to what follows.

In al-Khisal, from Yunus ibn Zabyan, who said: "Al-Sadiq Ja‘far ibn Muhammad (peace be upon him) said: 'Indeed, people worship Allah in three ways: one group worships Him out of desire for His reward—that is the worship of the greedy, which is covetousness; others worship Him out of fear of the Fire—that is the worship of slaves, which is dread; but I worship Him out of love for Him—that is the worship of the noble, and it is security (الأمن), because of His saying: وَهُمْ مِنْ فَزَعٍ يَوْمَئِذٍ آمِنُونَ — "and they will be secure from the terror of that day", and because of His saying: قُلْ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ تُحِبُّونَ اللهَ فَاتَّبِعُونِي يُحْبِبْكُمُ اللهُ وَيَغْفِرْ لَكُمْ ذُنُوبَكُمْ — "Say, ‘If you love Allah, then follow me; Allah will love you and forgive you your sins’" (3:31). So whoever loves Allah, Allah loves him; and whomever Allah loves is among the secure.'"

I say: The necessary consequence of the reasoning within it is the interpretation of the "good deed" (الحسنة) in the verse as the guardianship (الولاية) which is the worship of Allah the Exalted through the path of love, which necessitates the annihilation of the servant's will in His will, and the Exalted Himself taking charge of the servant's affair and directing him. This is one of the two meanings of the guardianship (ولاية) of ‘Ali (peace be upon him); for he (peace be upon him) is the master of guardianship and the first to open this door among the nation. Through this, most of the narrations stating that the "good deed" in the verse is the guardianship of ‘Ali (peace be upon him) can be interpreted.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Abu al-Shaykh, Ibn Mardawayh, and al-Daylami narrated from Ka‘b ibn ‘Ujrah, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله: regarding the saying of Allah: مَنْ جاءَ بِالْحَسَنَةِ فَلَهُ خَيْرٌ مِنْها — "Whoever brings a good deed shall receive [a reward] better than it": "He means by it the testimony that there is no god but Allah. And whoever brings an evil deed, He means by it polytheism (الشرك). It is said: this one saves, and that one ruins."

I say: This meaning is narrated from him صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله in different wordings through various chains. It is appropriate to qualify the interpretation of the "good deed" as "there is no god but Allah" with the rest of the legal rulings (الأحكام الشرعية) which are among the requirements of monotheism (التوحيد); otherwise, their legislation would be futile, and this is obvious.

In Tafsir al-Qummi, regarding His saying, the Exalted: إِنَّما أُمِرْتُ أَنْ أَعْبُدَ رَبَّ هذِهِ الْبَلْدَةِ الَّذِي حَرَّمَها — "I am only commanded to worship the Lord of this city, who has made it inviolable": He said: "Mecca."

In the same source, from his father, from Hammad ibn ‘Isa, from Hariz, from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him), who said: "When the Messenger of Allah صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله entered Mecca on the day he conquered it, he opened the door of the Kaaba and ordered the images in the Kaaba to be erased. Then he took hold of the two doorposts and said: 'Behold, Allah made Mecca inviolable on the day He created the heavens and the earth; so it is inviolable by the inviolability of Allah until the Day of Resurrection. Its game shall not be startled, its trees shall not be lopped, its fresh herbage shall not be cut, and its lost property is not lawful except for one who announces it.'"

Al-‘Abbas said: "O Messenger of Allah, except for lemon grass (الإذخر), for it is for the graves and the houses." The Messenger of Allah said: "Except for lemon grass."

I say: This is also narrated through Sunni chains.

In al-Durr al-Manthur: Ibn Mardawayh narrated from Ibn Mas‘ud, from the Prophet صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله, who said: "Whatever is in the Quran as وَمَا اللهُ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا تَعْمَلُونَ — 'And Allah is not oblivious of what you do' is with the ta (second person), and whatever is وَمَا رَبُّكَ بِغافِلٍ عَمَّا يَعْمَلُونَ — 'And your Lord is not oblivious of what they do' is with the ya (third person)."

Completed, and praise be to Allah.
